### 4.1 Hazard Identification Techniques

Identification of hazards is an essential step in guaranteeing the safety of robotic systems. This allows for the timely detection of possible dangers that could cause injury to humans, damage property, and slow or interrupt operations. A number of hazard identification techniques have been reported in the literature, including Unified Modeling Language, computerized risk assessment strategies, contextual risk assessment frameworks, and so forth.

#### 4.1.1 HAZOP-UML Method

It has been proven through study that the HAZOP-UML method can identify threats arising from human–robots interaction, especially during the robot development process (Guiochet [2016](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0051)). As presented in Figure [3](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-fig-0003), this method is based on two notations: hazard operability and Unified Modeling Language.



- i.
Unified modeling language: The graphical language known as Unified Modeling Language (UML) provides a standard method for creating system blueprints that include both concrete elements like database schemas, programming language statements, and reusable software components, and conceptual elements like company operations and system functions (Maylawati et al. [2018](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0088)). UML reinforces the initial stage of the development process by providing a comprehensible, uniform, and well-regulated visualization of the architecture and behavior of the robotic system.

- ii.
Hazard Operability (HAZOP): HAZOP is a generally accepted risk assessment method broadly utilized for industrial standardization. It offers a systematic approach to recognizing possible deviations in multifaceted operations, primarily applied in pharmaceutical, chemical, nuclear, oil, and gas industries (Choi and Byeon [2020](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0024)).


[![Details are in the caption following the image](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/a777b452-9383-45e8-981b-490e5413e7f9/rob70022-fig-0003-m.png)](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/d5ec2f42-ab82-472c-aa8b-e3afb649997a/rob70022-fig-0003-m.jpg)

**Figure 3**

[Open in figure viewer](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#) [PowerPoint](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/action/downloadFigures?id=rob70022-fig-0003&partId=&doi=10.1002%2Frob.70022)

HAZOP-UML process (Guiochet [2016](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0051)).

The combination of 2 methodologies – the HAZOP-UML method – enables safety professionals to thoroughly identify critical factors and assess risks. UML diagrams, like state machines, sequence diagrams, and communication models, are established to exemplify the architecture and interactions of a system. These diagrams are then examined using HAZOP guide words like “Less,” “More,” and “Reverse” to recognize deviations that could pose risks.

The implementation process involves several steps. First, the system is modeled using UML to produce detailed visual illustrations of the robotic system, centering on its interactions, states, and sequences. Afterward, potential deviations are identified using HAZOP techniques, and this was done to thoroughly analyze the causes and potential consequences. Mitigation strategies are afterward developed, including design modifications, redundancy measures, or safety controls. Iterative validations are performed to ensure that the suggested solutions effectively address the identified risks.

Safety evaluation examples additionally illustrate the application of the HAZOP-UML method. One instance is the situation of a robot-assisted dressing system for aged or physically challenged people (Delgado Bellamy et al. [2021](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0031)). The dressing assistance task involves uninterrupted physical robot and human interaction, where robots help users wear garments. The garment detection, grasping, and positioning tasks are managed using a Baxter robot, equipped with bi-manual operation.

The safety evaluation begins with creating UML diagrams to outline the operation of a robotic system. Sequence diagrams are used to represent the workflow of the dressing task, emphasizing interactions between the user and the robot. For example, the robot holding the garment at a specific angle for easy user access is demonstrated, and deviations like inappropriate positioning and delayed response are recognized using HAZOP guide words.

For each UML node, HAZOP analysis is applied using guide words, including “Commission,” “Omission,” and “Late.” This process recognizes potential threats, including:



- i.
Collision between the user and the arm of the robot during garment placement.

- ii.
Inappropriate grasping of the garment, which can injure or discomfort the user.

- iii.
Undue force applied by the robot during dressing, which can lead to physical injury.


To mitigate these risks, safety measures are proposed:



- a.
Integrating force sensors on the grippers of the robot to detect excessive pressure and adjust accordingly.

- b.
Executing real-time user feedback systems like touch-based inputs and voice commands to allow users to override the actions of the robot.

- c.
Designing adaptive control algorithms justification for user unpredictability – physical capabilities and different body sizes.


The continuous application of HAZOP-UML in this situation guarantees that safety risks are continuously checked and addressed throughout the development lifecycle of the robot. This approach underscores the importance of integrating hazard identification procedures into complex physical human and robot interaction tasks, where user safety is supreme.

This step-by-step approach is adopted by famous robot producers like KUKA Robotics, Robotsoft, and AIRBUS Corporation (Guiochet [2016](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0051)). For example, KUKA Robotics utilizes the HAZOP-UML technique to assess the risk of accidental robotic movements in robot and robot interaction situations. Through communication processes and modeling of state machines, risks, such as unintended state transitions are recognized and addressed via fail-safe protocols and redundant communication pathways. AIRBUS Corporation has used the HAZOP-UML method to augment the safety of autonomous systems, especially in situations connecting path planning and human–robot collaboration. Their implementation includes the identification of possible deviations in dynamic environments and detailed UML models to simulate likely interactions. This technique is applied to design adaptive control algorithms that check collisions and ensure smooth robot–human interactions. Likewise, Robotsoft adopted this method for enhancing the reliability of multi-robot communication modules. The company assessed risks associated with the delay in data transmission by applying HAZOP-UML communication models. Further, redundant communication systems and error-checking algorithms were executed to address these issues, guaranteeing the performance of their robotic systems in collaborative operations (Choi and Byeon [2020](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0024)).

The HAZOP-UML method has its limitations. It requires highly skilled workers to interpret UML diagrams and perform HAZOP analyses accurately. The process is time-consuming, particularly for multifaceted systems, and greatly relies on effective cooperation between safety and development experts. Moreover, the precision of the results greatly depends on the suitable description of system limits and risk parameters. Notwithstanding these challenges, the HAZOP-UML technique remains a multipurpose and robust approach for ensuring the safety of robotic systems, especially in integrated environments. By integrating organized modeling and systematic risk assessment, it provides a broad framework for detecting and mitigating threats throughout the development process.

Recent developments have advanced the application of the HAZOP-UML method. For example, the integration of generative AI like the HAZOP-GPT framework has been recommended to support human professionals in performing safety analyses of collaborative robotic systems (Hillen et al. [2024](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0057); Kranz et al. [2024](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0075)). This framework uses GPT-4 to help in detecting safety hazards, their causes, and possible solutions by automating specific aspects of the hazard detection process. It breaks down complicated tasks into simple assembly steps and uses guide words to produce possible risks systematically. This modernization approach has shown promise in augmenting the efficiency and accuracy of threat detection, particularly in dynamic human and robot collaboration environments.

### 4.2 Computerized Risk Assessment Strategies

A computerized solution was generated to establish generalized procedures for processing data related to threats and capacity, minimizing the influence of human bias in risk assessments (Koch [2019](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0073)). This strategy is designed to enhance objectivity and improve the reliability of risk evaluation procedures.

The computer-based solution developed connects output signals with the control safety input signals in a logical way. It establishes a logical link between the input factors and desired output, enabling automation of the risk assessment process in accordance with pre-specified conditions. The development of the input–output solution is created in the process-oriented safety state, guaranteeing that safety evaluations are integrated directly into the controller of the robotic system. Thus, this approach minimizes human errors and irregularities, improving the reliability of safety evaluations.

### 4.3 Contextual Risk Assessment Frameworks

A contextual risk evaluation framework has been developed for self-directed mobile robots (Müller et al. [2022](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0090)). This framework is established via the combined efforts of numerous teams specializing in antagonistic learning algorithms and serving as the foundation of the framework.

The developed framework utilizes learning adversary fault injectors and follows the system decision theory methodology. These antagonists describe destabilizing features within the system by introducing disturbances throughout the simulations. The key areas of contextual risk assessment include:



- i.
Dynamic disruption testing: By simulating dynamic disruptions, the framework assesses the capability of the system to tackle unanticipated challenges.

- ii.
Adversarial learning: The system learns to identify and correct faults caused by disturbances, leading to a comprehensive risk assessment.


A safe system can resist dynamic disturbances while sustaining operational reliability, and this approach allows for the active identification of faults and improves the applicability and performance of autonomous robots to real-world uncertainties.

### 4.4 System-Theoretic Process Analysis

System-theoretic process analysis is an iterative risk evaluation method for analyzing the cause of an accident during the design and development stage of robots to control safety hazards. System-theoretic process analysis explores the utilization of multiple controllers and reports possible disadvantages of using multilevel frameworks. The system-theoretic process analysis involves:



- i.
Hierarchical control structures: The hierarchical control system is a multi-layered framework designed to manage complex, large-scale industrial operations. At the lower level, local controllers interact directly with subsystems and carry out control tasks. This structure follows an algorithm approach to mitigate or eliminate risks, ranking control measures from the most effective and reliable to the least dependable.

- ii.
Control action analysis: Identifying unsafe control actions that could lead to hazards.

- iii.
Iterative cross-validation: This is the act of continuously authenticating limitations and risks via predefined standards and hazards mentioned in ISO 10218 (ISO International Organization for Standardization [2011](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0065)) and ISO 15066 (ISO International Organization for Standardization [2016](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0067)).


This approach enables experts to comprehend multifaceted activities resulting from the interaction of several system components and to execute safety procedures that mitigate these complexities.

### 4.5 Root Cause Analysis in Robot Accidents

Despite implementing control measures, the root causes of operator-injured robot accidents have been investigated to identify underlying issues (Lee et al. [2021](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0078)). An analysis of 369 robot accidents that caused injuries to operators in Korea from 2009 to 2019 revealed that approximately 30-40 robot accidents occur per year in Korea, predominantly in the manufacturing sector. This prompted researchers to question whether organization performs risk assessment activities correctly. Most Korean organizations use the 4 M technique of the Korea Occupational Safety and Health Agency (KOSHA) to undertake risk assessments (Kwon et al. [2010](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0076); Lee et al. [2021](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0078)). This approach evaluates hazards from four perspectives:



- i.
Man (People): Human factors, such as operator error, fatigue, or insufficient training.

- ii.
Machine (Technology): Equipment-related issues, including malfunctions, inadequate maintenance, or design flaws.

- iii.
Media (Environment): Environmental conditions like lighting, noise, or workspace layout that may affect safety.

- iv.
Management (Administration): Organizational policies, procedures, and safety culture.


Figure [4](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-fig-0004) classifies the risk assessment hazard categories for robots, highlighting both physical and nonphysical hazards. The sequential case analysis method was applied to identify the direct causes of robot fatalities, supplemented by interviews with safety experts. After examining the immediate reasons, the root cause analysis method delved into the underlying causes based on critical details such as:



- i.
Type of mishap: Nature of the accident (e.g., collision and entanglement).

- ii.
Methodology: Procedures or tasks being performed at the time of the accident.

- iii.
Region of damage: Specific body parts injured.


[![Details are in the caption following the image](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/8c56cb14-50e4-4c28-8d96-36210a33bd27/rob70022-fig-0004-m.png)](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/a982a95a-fd1b-476a-82f4-32e54837dfc8/rob70022-fig-0004-m.jpg)

**Figure 4**

[Open in figure viewer](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#) [PowerPoint](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/action/downloadFigures?id=rob70022-fig-0004&partId=&doi=10.1002%2Frob.70022)

Classification of the risk assessment hazard categories for robots, adopted from.

The investigation revealed that robot-related accidents are often instigated by misevaluation and omission of risk assessment items. Not only physical and visible hazards should be considered; it is crucial to account for nonphysical hazard factors such as personal, occupational, and system-related factors, as shown in Figure [4](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-fig-0004). Further analysis focused on identifying body parts most prone to injury in robot-related accidents (Kwon et al. [2010](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0076); Lee et al. [2021](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0078)). The susceptibility percentages are as follows:



- a.
Arm: 7%.

- b.
Leg/Foot: 7%.

- c.
Abdomen/Back: 9%.

- d.
Neck/Chest: 22%.

- e.
Head/Face: 24%.

- f.
Hand/Finger: 31%.


These statistics can guide robot manufacturers in optimizing designs to incorporate inherent safety features, particularly in areas interfacing with highly susceptible body parts. Additionally, it emphasizes the importance of appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE) for operators.

### 4.6 Hrzard Ranking Number (HRN) Method

Hippertt et al. ([2019](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0058)) introduced a method for estimating the required safety level when deploying a robot, classifying it as acceptable or unsuitable for interdisciplinary teams based on computational results (Figure [5](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-fig-0005)). The HRN was used to quantify the safety calculation process, establishing intervals for risk levels.

[![Details are in the caption following the image](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/bf4d9c65-def7-44cf-ba8d-40c5b238b773/rob70022-fig-0005-m.png)](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/ddc90bdb-d994-4e9d-854a-ceb73267c09b/rob70022-fig-0005-m.jpg)

**Figure 5**

[Open in figure viewer](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#) [PowerPoint](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/action/downloadFigures?id=rob70022-fig-0005&partId=&doi=10.1002%2Frob.70022)

HRN variables and HRN variable values adopted from (Hippertt et al. [2019](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0058)).

The HRN is calculated using four primary variables:



- i.
LO (Likelihood of occurrence): The probability that a hazard will occur, rated on a scale (e.g., rare to frequent).

- ii.
FE (Frequency of exposure): How often individuals are exposed to the hazard (e.g., daily and weekly).

- iii.
DPH (Degree of possible harm): The potential severity of harm, indicating the seriousness of potential hazards (e.g., minor injury to fatality).

- iv.
NP (Number of persons at risk): The number of individuals who could be affected by the hazard. The HRN is calculated using Equation [1](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-disp-0001 "Link to equation").


HRN=LO×FE×DPH×NP(1)

HRN values are categorized to classify the danger level of operations:



- a.
Negligible: HRN < 1.

- b.
Acceptable: HRN between 1 and 20.

- c.
Undesirable: HRN between 20 and 70.

- d.
Intolerable: HRN between 70 and 1000.

- e.
Unacceptable: HRN ≥ 1000.


These groups serve as procedures. It can be adopted for different types of robots to enhance the applicability of the HRN method, such as industrial collaborative robots, professional service robots, robotic devices, personal service robots, industrial robots, and mobile robots. By calculating risks, organizations can prioritize safety measures effectively (Hippertt et al. [2019](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0058)).

#### 4.6.1 Practical Applications of the Hazard Rating Number (HRN) Method

The HRN method is a simple technique for systematically evaluating work-related hazards and ranking risk management efforts. By combining key parameters like frequency, likelihood, severity, and the number of persons affected, the HRN method provides a simple yet well-structured framework for assessing safety risks in a workplace.

To demonstrate the application of HRN-based risk assessment, two case studies were examined:



- i.
Bucket-Wheel Excavator Maintenance in Mining Operations (Žunjić and Igor [2021](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0141)).

- ii.
Shipyard Floating Dock Operations (Bayhun and Demirel [2024](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0006)).


These case studies emphasize the usefulness and practical significance of the HRN method in different industrial environments, highlighting its value as a technique for enhancing operational sustainability and workplace safety.

### 4.7 Case Study 1: Bucket-Wheel Excavator Maintenance Worker

A detailed risk evaluation was carried out for maintenance personnel operating bucket-wheel excavators in Serbia's Kolubara Mining Basin (Žunjić and Igor [2021](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0141)). These machines present distinctive work-related hazards due to their operational complexity, scale, and environmental issues. Maintenance experts face amplified risks, such as noise, exposure to dust, vibration, and ergonomic stress from awkward working positions. The HRN method was adopted to detect and assess risks across key classifications, assigning numerical values to risk parameters like exposure, likelihood, and severity. This technique enabled a numerical prioritization of hazards, enlightening risk minimization strategies.

Below are some key findings:



- i.
Dust exposure – Very high risk (HRN = 128): Continued exposure to airborne particles in an enclosed environment increases the possibility of respiratory disorders.

- ii.
Vibration and noise – High risk for both (HRN = 64): Prolonged exposure to high-decibel vibrations and noise caused musculoskeletal disorders and damage to the eardrum.

- iii.
Ergonomic strain – Significant risk (HRN = 16): The narrowed and bad postures required during maintenance operations led to long-term musculoskeletal issues.

- iv.
Manual handling of heavy equipment – Significant risk (HRN = 20): Lifting heavy equipment and components like reducer units caused undue physical stress on workers.

- v.
Exposure to rotating machine parts – Significant risk (HRN = 40): Maintenance conducted without proper barriers exposed workers to potential injuries from moving parts.


The following actions were suggested to minimize these risks:



- i.
Dust control: Install dust extraction ventilation systems in workspaces.

- ii.
Noise and vibration management: Provide personal protective equipment, such as anti-vibration gloves and noise-canceling earmuffs.

- iii.
Ergonomic training: Conduct sensitization workshops on safe lifting procedures and make available mechanical aids.

- iv.
Safety barriers: Use interlocked guards for rotating parts to prevent contact during maintenance.

- v.
Workforce monitoring: Regular medical checkups for personnel exposed to high-risk environments.


### 4.8 Case Study 2: Sustainable Shipyard Floating Dock Operations

Bayhun and Demirel ([2024](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0006)) investigated work-related risks in shipyard floating dock operations, a sector characterized by high-risk operations involving heavy machinery, complex emergency scenarios, and confined spaces. The study prioritizes hazards and enhances emergency preparedness, advancing sustainable and safe management in the workplace. The study employed the fuzzy combined compromise solution and spherical fuzzy analytical hierarchy process to evaluate risks. This model integrated the HRN and Fine–Kinney methods and 4 key risk factors: frequency, probability, number of people at risk, and consequence.

The dock captains and safety managers performed pairwise comparisons to evaluate the weights of risk criteria. Thus, 13 work-related hazards were identified and ranked based on their scores. Here are the key findings:



- i.
Insufficient access to the pool area – Highest priority (Score = 3.553): Employees lacked suitable pathways during emergencies, delaying response times and increasing risk.


- a.
Restricted crane movement (Score = 3.391): Congestion in docking pools limited crane accessibility, hampering operations.

- b.
Emergency crew dispatch challenges (Score = 3.344): Delays in dispatching emergency teams were attributed to poor communication and insufficient equipment.

- c.
Restricted intervention areas – Lowest priority (Score = 1.803): Obstructions in floating dock areas limited intervention but were deemed less critical compared to other hazards.


The study provided specific corrective and preventive measures for the top-ranked hazards:



- i.
Access to pool areas: Ensure unrestricted access to docks and ramps, with emergency pathways kept clear at all times.

- ii.
Crane movement: Evaluate trim and slope conditions for safe crane operation and ensure adequate docking space.

- iii.
Emergency response: Institute communication procedures, conduct emergency sensitization, and equip shipyards with essential rescue tools.


The findings underscore the significance of incorporating advanced fuzzy decision-making models to mitigate complicated work-related safety challenges in high-risk industries like shipyards. The comparison between risk assessment methods is presented in Table [1](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-tbl-0001 "Link to table").

| Criterion/Method | Focus | Best For | Style | Complexity | User expertise | Scalability | Advantage | Limitations |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| HRN | Prioritizes risk numerically. | Industrial robots | P | L | L | H | Fast and easy prioritization | Oversimplifies complex risks |
| FMEA | Identifies component failures. | Design and maintenance | P | M | M | M | Improves component design | Time-intensive |
| FTA | Traces root causes of failures. | High-stakes applications | R | H | H | L | Clear visualization of causes | Requires detailed knowledge |
| HAZOP-UML | Visualizes early-stage risks. | Human-robot interfaces | P | H | H | L | Standardized early-stage risk | Depends on the team's expertise |
| STPA | Analyzes system interactions. | Collaborative robotics | P | H | H | H | Iterative validation of hazards | Relies on predefined standards |
| RCA | Investigates accident causes. | Postaccident analysis | R | M | M | M | Highlights systemic flaws | Focused on postaccident cases |
| Contextual Frameworks | Simulates faults dynamically. | Autonomous robots in dynamic environments | P | H | H | H | Enhances fault resilience | Resource-intensive simulations |
| Computerized Strategies | Automates risk quantification. | Large-scale industrial systems | P | M | M | H | Reduces human bias | The initial setup is costly |

- Abbreviation: FMEA, Failure Mode and Effects Analysis; FTA, Fault Tree Analysis; H, High; HRN, Hazard Ranking Number; HAZOP, Hazard and Operability Study; L, Low, M, Medium; P, Proactive, R, Reactive; RCA, Root Cause Analysis; STPA, System-Theoretic Process Analysis; UML, Unified Modeling Language.

### 4.9 Practical Engineering Solutions

Real-world engineering solutions play a critical role in alleviating documented hazards, especially hazards associated with mechanical risks in industrial robots.

#### 4.9.1 Mechanical Safety Couplings

A mechanical safety coupling was developed for industrial robots to reduce the risk of harm caused by moving robots (Lämmle [2019](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0077)). This development offers an evident response to known threats by decreasing the energy transmitted during the collision. The essential components of a mechanical safety coupling include a disconnection mechanism and magnetorheological fluid. The disconnection mechanism feature disengages the elements between the robot and tool upon collision, resulting in a reduction in the impact force transferred to the operator. On the other hand, the magnetorheological fluid serves as an inherent safety clutch on a coupling, rapidly adjusting its viscosity with the help of a magnetic field for quick disengagement when required.

The incorporation of a mechanical safety coupling into the risk management and evaluation framework underscores the complementary roles of technological solutions and analytical methods. This approach highlights the connection between practical technological innovations and theoretical risk assessment solutions, closing the gap between real-time application and analytical solutions.

#### 4.9.2 Velocity Estimation Algorithms

A step-by-step method for estimating the allowable velocities according to the movement and position of operators relative to a robot system was developed by Ganglbauer et al. ([2020](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0039)). The technique calculates the safe speed limits as the greatest allowable forces defined in global standards like ISO 10218 and ISO/TS 15066. The essential features of the method include:



- i.
Dynamic monitoring: Uninterruptedly measuring the position of the body parts susceptible to hazard and operator.

- ii.
Force and velocity control: Adjust the robot's applied forces and velocities in real time to stay within safe limits.

- iii.
Compliance with standards: Ensuring that interactions adhere to verified safety standards during human–robot interaction.


Scientists can establish procedures to regulate velocities and applied forces, enhancing safety during robot-human interactions. Several key factors must be considered when designing a robot-human collaborative system (Figure [6](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-fig-0006)), including:



- a.
Human factors: These include the behavior of the operator, closeness, and vulnerability to injury.

- b.
Robot characteristics: Speed and mass of the robot, and control systems.

- c.
Environmental conditions: Workspace layout, presence of obstacles, and environmental hazards.

- d.
Safety protocols: Emergency stop functions, safety-rated monitored stops, and protective equipment.

## 5 Safety Models, Algorithms, and Functions

The safety feature is a vital element of robotics systems, predominantly when there is interaction between humans and robots. Various procedures and techniques have been developed to recognize unsafe situations to ensure an efficient operation and safety of the people, design safety routes, and monitor the work environment.

In this section, different safety functions, models, and algorithms deployed in robotics were discussed. Emphasizes were laid on the major advancement and their usefulness in human–robot interactive systems, and the section is grouped into 7 (Bdiwi [2014](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0007); Davoodi et al. [2021](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0030); Gleirscher et al. [2022](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0044); Li and Savkin [2018](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0080); Sreenivas and Rao [2022](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0112); Sun, Li, et al. [2022](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0116); Wescoat et al. [2022](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0129)):



- i.
Motion Planning and Trajectory Control.

- ii.
Safety in Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM).

- iii.
Collision Avoidance.

- iv.
Fault Identification.

- v.
Safety Applications of Augmented Reality, Virtual Reality, and Mixed Reality.

- vi.
Safety Concerns of Reinforcement Learning.

- vii.
Safety Control System Verification and Validation.


### 5.1 Motion Planning and Trajectory Control

Safe and efficient motion planning is essential for robots operating in human interferences atmospheres. Several procedures have been established and documented to reduce accidents and improve performance. A method for creating safe and effective trajectory plans was developed using a novel spline-like curve to enable smooth mobility and minimize jerk motions (Rojas et al. [2020](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0103)). This technique reduced the system efficiency by about 19.6% and 13.1% compared to the jerk smoothness and default settings of the robot, respectively. In another study conducted by Binchi et al. ([2021](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0014)), a system that predicts the upper body and arms of an operator utilizing the combination of formal models and data-driven techniques following interval arithmetic was recommended to ensure smooth robot operation in human interference atmospheres. The recommended system demonstrates strong resistance to noise and requires less hardware. Integrating this high-tech solution into robot planning algorithms can significantly improve industrial safety.

A spectrum of trajectories in dynamic settings has been defined using the probabilistic movement primitives tool (Davoodi et al. [2021](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0030)). This method was adopted to predict the probabilistic movements of people in their surroundings. The robot was then directed along a programmed trajectory while maintaining a safe distance from the people, as predicted by the motion dispersion of probabilistic movement primitives. The safety of the operation was achieved via the combined effect of control barrier functions and predictive model control (Wang, Wang, et al. [2023](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0128)).

Sun, Shan, et al. ([2022](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0115)) developed a control method for a padded robot that recognizes random movement and internal distractions caused by changes in mass and posture of the operator. This robot was found helpful, especially in assisting the elderly, disabled, and people who suffer from injury from fatal accidents in their daily activities. The method used in restricting the trajectory and velocity of a cushion robot can be applied to other robot varieties that coexist with people to improve the safety of the people.

### 5.2 Safety in Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)

SLAM is one of the most innovative positioning techniques in robotics (Abaspur Kazerouni et al. [2022](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0001); Alsadik and Karam [2021](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0004); Tiwari and Young Chong [2020](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0120)). It maps out an unexplored area and detects the exact position of the sensor within the map, with distinctive consideration for real-time operation. Numerous techniques for solving and articulating the theoretical limitation of SLAM have been studied (Chen et al. [2022](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0023); Wang et al. [2023](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0128)). These techniques have been used in diverse outdoor and indoor domains (Chan et al. [2018](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0018); Lu et al. [2023](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0084)).

The SLAM technique has found applications in various industrial settings. For example, automated guided vehicles and autonomous mobile robots majorly depend on SLAM algorithms for industrial floor safety and collision avoidance (Alsadik and Karam [2021](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0004); Mur-Artal and Tardos [2017](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0091); Ramadhan et al. [2021](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0098); Scaradozzi et al. [2018](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0105); D. Zhu et al. [2019](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0139)).

Also, ORB-SLAM2 can relocalize, close loops, and reuse its map in real-time applications using conventional central processing units, and it found applications in several settings, including drones flying around industrial areas (Mur-Artal and Tardos [2017](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0091)). This system uses RGB-D cameras, which provide important information for the implementation of relocalizing, closing looping, and reusing maps.

It has been demonstrated through research that the combination of the SLAM technique and RGB-D cameras can improve the safety efficiency of a mining robot (Zhu et al. [2019](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0139)). The mining surroundings present distinctive challenges, which make mitigating issues, such as weight degradation and particle dilution important. Examinations through the MATLAB software revealed that the adaptive closed-loop firefly algorithm-probabilistic SLAM method performed better than the traditional firefly computationally efficient. This method enhanced the exactness of location determination for robots, thus improving environmental safety (see Figure [7](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-fig-0007)).

[![Details are in the caption following the image](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/f31901cd-99ff-4886-bc86-78703c5c3f18/rob70022-fig-0007-m.png)](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/0bda980f-f0cd-4785-a9fa-4b31995d3739/rob70022-fig-0007-m.jpg)

**Figure 7**

[Open in figure viewer](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#) [PowerPoint](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/action/downloadFigures?id=rob70022-fig-0007&partId=&doi=10.1002%2Frob.70022)

Results obtained from simulation using SLAM: (a) FA-PF-SLAM simulation and (b) ACLFA-PF-SLAM simulation (Zhu et al. [2019](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0139)).

The utilization of robotics in complicated surgical situations is becoming popular in the medical sector (Bravi et al. [2023](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0016); Gul [2022](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0052); Jacobson and Pandya [2023](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0069); Yamout et al. [2023](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0132)). Recently, several surgical specializations have integrated new marginally invasive technology (Ferraguti et al. [2020](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0037); Russo [2020](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0104); Scaradozzi et al. [2018](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0105); Takagi et al. [2023](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0117); Till et al. [2023](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0119); Wang, Chen, et al. [2023](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0126)). The precision and accuracy of the robotics developed to support medical experts, particularly surgeons, can be improved by incorporating SLAM techniques in the robotics systems (Chen et al. [2018](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0022); Wang, Chen, et al. [2023](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0126)).

SLAM also found application in image-guided surgery under the assumption of a static environment. Visual SLAM systems can precisely convert the trajectory of a surgeon into a real-time activity of the surgical robot in the patient using sensing devices or similar tools (Scaradozzi et al. [2018](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0105)). The reports available in the literature have shown that unscented Kalman filters are the SLAM techniques that are popularly used (Chatterjee and Matsuno [2007](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0020); Ferraguti et al. [2020](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0037); Lekkala and Mittal [2016](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0079); Song et al. [2022](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0111); Wu et al. [2018](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0130)).

#### 5.2.1 Performance Metrics for SLAM Safety Implementation

The performance evaluation of SLAM systems is critical for their utilization in critical safety applications, including delivery robots, autonomous vehicles, industrial robots, and medical robots, among others. Several numerical metrics enable in-depth assessment, such as real-time performance, reliability, localization accuracy, and robustness scalability to environmental variations. These metrics combine to ensure the operational feasibility of the system under different conditions.

#### 5.2.1.1 Localization Accuracy

Localization accuracy remains fundamental, quantifying the ability of the SLAM system to estimate the pose of a robot with minimal deviation from the exact trajectory. Relative pose and absolute trajectory errors are widely utilized for this purpose, with root mean square error as a common measure. Dynamic-aware frameworks, like DynaSLAM-II, enhance their accuracy by filtering out moving objects, while advanced systems, such as ORB-SLAM3, achieve sub-decimeter accuracy in controlled environments (Bescos et al. [2021](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0012); Mur-Artal and Tardos [2017](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0091); W. Zhou et al. [2024](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0138)).

#### 5.2.1.2 Real-Time Performance

Real-time performance is unavoidable for systems operating in dynamic environments. Metrics, such as latency and frames per second, assess the responsiveness and speed of the SLAM pipeline. Real-time systems (NICE-SLAM) constantly achieve processing speeds above 30 frames per second, meeting the standards for autonomous navigation. Latency under 50 ms guarantees suitable decision-making and system safety (Z. Zhu et al. [2022](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0140)).

#### 5.2.1.3 Reliability

Reliability metrics assess the robustness of the SLAM system under unstable conditions, including lighting changes, sensor noise, and dynamic obstacles. The failure rate and robustness index are generally used indicators. Semantic-aware systems like DynaSLAM-II exhibit robustness index values above 90%, guaranteeing reliable performance in metropolitan cities with dynamic elements (Bescos et al. [2021](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0012)).

#### 5.2.1.4 Map Quality

The quality of the generated map is essential for applications requiring continuous navigation or interaction with the environment. Map consistency, completeness, and resolution metrics are generally adopted to evaluate the fidelity of the reconstructed environment. For example, ElasticFusion achieves high-resolution compressed mapping with millimeter accuracy. This makes it suitable for robotic inspection manipulation (Park et al. 2022).

#### 5.2.1.5 Computational Efficiency

Efficiency metrics center on resource utilization, such as memory usage, energy consumption, CPU, and GPU load. These parameters are critical for the implementation of SLAM on embedded systems with limited computational power, such as drones and handheld devices. Significant reductions in resource requirements with higher accuracy are achieved using lightweight SLAM algorithms and this enhanced utilization in constrained environments (Jung et al. 2024).

#### 5.2.1.6 Scalability

Scalability measures the ability of a system to handle long-duration operations and large-scale environments without a reduction in performance. The commonly used metrics include the capacity to integrate new benchmarks without excessive computational overhead and processing time per frame in large-scale maps. Large-scale SLAM implementations like NICE-SLAM demonstrate scalability by efficiently managing maps of indoor environments larger than 1000 m2 (Zhu et al. [2022](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0140)).

#### 5.2.1.7 Robustness to Environmental Variations

Environmental robustness evaluates the ability of a system to perform consistently under varying conditions, such as changes in lighting, weather, and dynamic scenes. Metrics include system performance under high-dynamic-range lighting or low-texture surfaces. Semantic-aware SLAM approaches leverage machine learning models to adapt to such variations, achieving robust operation in previously unseen environments (Singh et al. 2022).

#### 5.2.1.8 Loop Closure and Drift Correction

The ability to identify and correct loop closure meaningfully impacts the long-term performance of the system. The effectiveness of the SLAM system in recognizing previously visited locations is evaluated using loop closure recall and precision metrics. Systems, such as lRTAB-Map, recorded recall rates above 95%, enhancing global consistency and reducing drift (Labbé and Michaud [2019](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0151); Zhang et al. [2022](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0135)).

#### 5.2.1.9 Resilience to Sensor Noise and Failures

In critical safety situations, failures and resilience to sensor noise are key performance metrics. To assess this capability, recovery time from sensor dropout and error propagation under simulated sensor noise are the metrics employed. Multi-sensor SLAM systems demonstrate higher resilience by fusing data from multiple modalities, such as LiDAR, cameras, and IMUs (H. Zhang et al. 2024).

### 5.3 Collision Avoidance

Collision avoidance is a key component of robot navigation and control that guarantees the safe operation of robots in human environments, other robotic agents, and moving obstacles. The implementation and design of collision avoidance systems need to be done with precise mathematical modeling and sophisticated algorithmic methods, balancing safety, efficiency, and robustness. Robot systems move within specified velocity and acceleration limits to guarantee smooth and safe motion. These constraints are mathematically expressed and presented in Equation [2](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-disp-0002 "Link to equation").



\|\|v(t)\|\|≤vmax; \|\|a(t)\|\|≤amax(2)

where v(t) and a(t) represent the robot's velocity and acceleration vectors at time t, and vmax and amax define the maximum allowable velocity and acceleration.

Satisfying these constraints reduces the likelihood of sudden or dangerous maneuvers, thereby eliminating the risk of collision and ensuring the stability of the robot (Gunathillake et al. [2019](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0053); Lin et al. [2022](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0082)).

One fundamental condition for collision avoidance is to keep a proper separation distance from a possible threat. The minimum separation distance dmin is a boundary in the sense that any decrease below it represents the onset of an imminent collision. This distance is dynamically evaluated using Equation [3](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-disp-0003 "Link to equation").



dsep=\|\|pr−po\|\|(3)

where pr and po are the position vectors of the robot and the obstacle, respectively. Ensuring that dsep≥dmin is critical for safety.

For dynamic environments, predictive modeling becomes essential to approximate future positions of moving obstacles and proactively adjust the robot's trajectory, enabling a proactive approach to collision avoidance (Zeng et al. [2021](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0134)).

Trajectory planning is at the heart of collision avoidance and is often formulated as an optimization problem that minimizes travel costs while satisfying safety constraints. The objective is to determine a smooth and efficient trajectory x(t) by minimizing a cost function (Equation [4](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-disp-0004 "Link to equation")).



J=∫0T(\|\|v(t)\|\|2+α\|\|a(t)\|\|2)dt(4)

where α is a weight factor balancing velocity and acceleration minimization, and T is the planning horizon.

The optimization is subject to constraints including \|\|v(t)\|\|≤vmax,\|\|a(t)\|\|≤amax,dsep≥dmin, and boundary conditions such as p(0)=pstart and p(T)=pgoal. Solving this optimization yields a trajectory that respects the constraints while minimizing energy consumption and ensuring safety (Li and Savkin [2018](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0080)).

Artificial Potential Field methods offer an intuitive yet powerful approach to collision avoidance. This method models the environment of a robot as a field of forces that guide it toward its goal while repelling it from obstacles. The total force acting on the robot is calculated using Equations [5](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-disp-0005 "Link to equation") and [6](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-disp-0006 "Link to equation").



Ftotal=Fattract+Frepel;Fattract=katt(pgoal−pr)(5)

Frepel=krep1dsep−1dminpr−po\|\|pr−po\|\|3,ifdsep≤dmin0,ifdsep>dmin(6)

where katt is the attraction constant, pgoal is the goal position, pr is the robot's current position, krep is the repulsion constant, Frepel is the repulsive force, po is the position of the obstacle in the environment, dsep is separation distance between the robot and obstacle, dmin is the minimum allowable separation distance between the robot and the obstacle, and Fattract is the attractive force.

The combined forces dynamically steer the robot along a collision-free path while maintaining its trajectory toward the goal (Zhen et al. [2022](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0136)).

Simulation technologies improve collision avoidance strategies. Simulation offers a safe environment for testing and tuning algorithms. Real-time model building and predictive analysis are leveraged by the simulations to improve operation variables like separation distances, dynamic forces, and allowable velocities. Modeling the interaction of the robot with its environment allows scientists to calculate the best conditions for productivity and safety. Predictive models are very useful in estimating the future position of obstacles and adjusting the robot's trajectory pre-emptively (Miro et al. [2022](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0089)).

With more robots sharing the same environment, collision avoidance becomes more complicated. Dynamic controllers using recurrent neural networks help analyze the velocities and distances between robots and change their paths accordingly to coordinate properly. These controllers let robots collaborate in factories without crashing into each other. Optimization techniques such as the Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm are effective in predicting and preventing collisions during human–robot interaction. They achieve this through the continuous adjustment of the robot's trajectory through real-time sensor input (He et al. [2021](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0055); Sharkawy and Mostfa [2021](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0107)).

Advanced learning methods like reinforcement learning (RL) and imitation learning have been used in collision avoidance. RL allows robots to learn the best means of motion by trying out options, rewarding safe and efficient routes, and punishing dangerous actions. Imitation learning enables robots to learn from expert behavior to navigate without collisions in complicated environments. These methods are supported by strong math tools and predictions, which improve the reliability and resilience of robotic systems (Gunathillake et al. [2019](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0053)). Its relevance in modern robotics is highlighted by the creation and use of advanced algorithms for collision avoidance.

Through the use of velocity and acceleration constraints and safe separation distances, as well as advanced optimization and learning-based techniques, robots can safely and efficiently move through complex and dynamic environments. This holistic approach to collision avoidance makes robots dependable, flexible, and useful in various applications, ranging from manufacturing to safety-critical applications like aiding in disaster situations and working together with humans.

### 5.4 Fault Detection

Failure detection is a critical task in many engineering and technological systems. Integrating machine learning has enhanced fault detection by improving reliability and reducing the rate of equipment failures. Equipment producers have incorporated machine learning to detect equipment faults to eradicate unanticipated robot reactions due to defective components (Dong et al. [2023](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0033); Giel and Dabrowska [2023](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0041)). Among the prominent approaches, hybrid gradient boosting has been identified to detect failure in robotic arms. Utilizing the combined effect of machine learning and statistical modeling is referred to as hybrid gradient boosting (Costa et al. [2019](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0027)).

Wescoat et al. ([2022](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0129)) conducted an algorithm comparison study, testing models including random forest regression, support vector regression, and deep neural network (DNN) regression on contrived failure data for a 6-degree-of-freedom joint collaborative robot as seen in Table [2](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-tbl-0002 "Link to table"). The report showed that little over-fitting, bias, and deep neural network regression was the best model with 98% accuracy. Fault diagnosis typically begins with feature extraction, where raw sensor data is transformed into useable representation. Some commonly used techniques are Wavelet transformation and Principal Component Analysis (Equation [7](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-disp-0007 "Link to equation")). With Wavelet transformation, it performs a time-frequency analysis by decomposing signals into scaled and shifted wavelets. It also helps reduce data dimensionality while retaining significant variance, enabling efficient fault classification.



WΨ(a,b)=∫−∞∞x(t)Ψa,b(t)dt(7)

| Criteria | Random forest regression (RFR) | Support vector regression (SVR) | Deep neural network regression (DNNR) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Strengths | - – <br>  Robust to overfitting<br>  <br>- – <br>  Computationally efficient during training<br>  <br>- – <br>  Handles nonlinear relationships effectively | - – <br>  Excels with small data sets<br>  <br>- – <br>  Balances bias and variance with optimal hyperparameter tuning | - – <br>  Ideal for high-dimensional and complex data sets<br>  <br>- – <br>  Excels in hierarchical feature extraction and generalization |
| Weaknesses | - – <br>  Struggles with scalability and complex fault scenarios<br>  <br>- – <br>  Moderate generalization ability | - – <br>  Computationally expensive training<br>  <br>- – <br>  Less suitable for large data sets due to kernel evaluations | - – <br>  Computationally intensive<br>  <br>- – <br>  Requires significant memory and specialized hardware for training |
| Performance | 85% testing accuracy: Notable overfitting under varying fault data distributions. | ~96-98% testing accuracy: Reduced overfitting compared to RFR | ~98% testing accuracy: Minimal bias and variance (most reliable among approaches tested) |
| Training time | ~30 min: Scales linearly with data set size | ~60 min: Increases significantly with kernel complexity | ~120 min- highest among the approaches due to iterative optimization across multiple layers |
| Inference time | Fast: Predictions involve traversing decision trees | Moderate: Kernel evaluations increase inference time | Slower than RFR- Dominated by matrix multiplications during the forward pass |
| Memory consumption | Lightweight: Suitable for embedded systems | High: Kernel matrices grow with data set size | Significant- Requires GPUs or high-performance CPUs but can be optimized through pruning and quantization |
| Energy efficiency | Highly energy-efficient during both training and inference | Moderate energy consumption, particularly during training | Least energy-efficient during training; comparable to SVR during optimized inference |
| Suitability for real-time applications | Well-suited due to fast inference and low computational demands | Limited suitability for large-scale real-time tasks due to training complexity | Suitable with optimizations; pruning and quantization enable compatibility with real-time systems |

where WΨ(a,b) is the wavelet transform coefficient, a and b are the scaling and translation parameters, x is the signal, and Ψ(t) is the mother wavelet. The deep neural network employed by Wescoat et al. ([2022](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0129)) consisted of an input layer that encodes extracted features and six hidden layers with 64 nodes each using Rectified linear unit activation functions. The output layer employs a softmax activation function for fault classification.

The training of DNNs requires careful tuning of hyperparameters to ensure efficient learning and high accuracy in fault detection tasks. For the model used, the learning rate was set at 0.001, to set a balance between convergence speed and stability during training. A batch size of 64 was used to process subsets of the training data during each forward and backward pass, enabling efficient computation while mitigating memory constraints. To optimize the weights of the network, the Adam optimizer was utilized. This optimizer is known for its adaptability and computational efficiency and adjusts learning rates dynamically for each parameter based on moment estimates of gradients.

The fault classification criterion in the DNN was designed to identify faults based on predicted probabilities from the softmax activation function in the output layer. A fault f was classified when the predicted probability for a given class k met or exceeded a predefined confidence threshold τ, expressed in Equations [8](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-disp-0008 "Link to equation") and [9](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-disp-0009 "Link to equation").



P(y=k\|x)=ezk∑j=1Kezk(8)

P(y=k\|x)≥τ(9)

where zk is the input to the k-th neuron, x is the input feature vector (extracted features from the sensor data used for fault diagnosis), y is the class label assigned to the input x, K is the total number of fault classes, P(y=k\|x) is the probability that input x belongs to class k, and τ=0.9 is the confidence threshold.

This threshold ensures that the model only makes classifications when it is sufficiently confident in its predictions, thereby reducing the likelihood of false positives in critical fault detection scenarios.

The computational efficiency of the DNN, especially in real-time applications, is heavily influenced by the matrix multiplications carried out during the forward pass. Among different approaches toward optimization for enabling feasibility with real-time deployment, some approaches include model pruning and quantization. The model pruning methodology was aimed at reducing network size by removing unnecessary connections and weights, while quantization further enhanced efficiency by using lower precision formats for weights and activations. These measures reduced computational demands and memory usage, thus allowing the DNN to accurately and reliably perform fault detection even on resource-constrained systems.

Landgrebe and Duin reported a 98% accuracy; it does not include confusion matrices or receiver operating characteristic curves to substantiate this result (Landgrebe and Duin, 2008). These metrics, commonly used for evaluating classification models, could provide deeper insights into true positive, true negative, false positive, and false negative rates. Nevertheless, the work presented comparable training (99%) and testing (98%) accuracies, alongside careful hyperparameter tuning and cross-validation. These steps may reduce the likelihood of overfitting and strengthen the validity of the reported performance. Future research, as proposed by the authors, could further validate the model's robustness by applying it to diverse data sets and additional fault scenarios.

Fan et al. ([2022](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0036)) introduced another notable approach to fault detection and management, focusing on ducted-fan aerial robots with fractional actuator failures. This fault detection system adopts frequency domain analysis to identify signal anomalies indicative of actuator faults, such as deviations in motor currents and vibration patterns. The system detects faults using signal processing techniques that analyze the dynamic behavior of the actuator. Specifically, the system calculates the frequency response of the signals using the Fourier transform (Equation [10](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-disp-0010 "Link to equation")):



F(ω)=∫−∞∞x(t)e−jωtdt(10)

where x(t) is the time-domain signal, ω is the angular frequency, and F(ω) represents the frequency components.

The computed frequency spectrum is compared with predefined fault signatures, and any deviation beyond threshold limits is considered indicative of faults. These thresholds are dynamically tuned based on operational conditions for accurate fault identification.

To mitigate the impact of actuator degradation, Fan et al. employed an adaptive control strategy that dynamically adjusted system parameters through a robust baseline loop. The adaptive control law is described in Equation [11](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-disp-0011 "Link to equation"):



u(t)=u0(t)+Kf·Δu(11)

where u(t) is the total control input, u₀(t) represents the baseline control input for nominal conditions, Kf is the fault compensation gain, and Δu is the adjustment term calculated to redistribute control efforts.

The compensation gain Kf is adaptively updated to redistribute the control load to unaffected actuators, thereby minimizing the impact of the fault. This mechanism also incorporates a fault estimation model, which predicts the degree of actuator degradation using an error signal e(t) (Equation [12](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-disp-0012 "Link to equation")):



e(t)=ydesired(t)−yactual(t)(12)

where ydesired(t) is the desired system output, and yactual(t) is the measured output. The error signal is used to dynamically adjust control parameters, ensuring stability and desired performance.

These low-latency algorithms and embedded control strategies in combination will afford the system fault tolerance in real time. These approaches minimize the computational delays of the signal processing and adaptive control calculations, therefore providing compatibility with onboard processors. This approach not only reduces downtime and improves reliability but also shows great potential in dynamic and high-stakes applications, such as disaster response and UAV operations. These methodologies, supported by robust mathematical frameworks, can help highlight the transformational role of advanced fault detection and control systems to improve the safety and resilience of robotic systems.

### 5.5 Safety Applications of Augmented Reality, Virtual Reality, and Mixed Reality

The integration of innovative technologies like augmented reality (AR), virtual reality (VR), and mixed reality in robotic safety systems has become popular in the last few years. These technological innovations have opened new approaches to improve collaboration, safety, and efficiency in the robotic field. An internet-based solution that simulates real robot–human interactions was examined for speed gains in controlling fabric when manufacturing hybrid material assemblies for aerospace (Vosniakos et al. [2019](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0123)). According to the report, it was observed that reducing the velocity of the robot occasionally changed the direction of the robot and sporadically taking no action proved successful.

Ramadhan et al. ([2021](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0098)) utilized SLAM to create an augmented reality smartphone application for furniture visualization. The study attempted to observe furniture products from an advertisement using augmented reality monitoring. AR technology is an augmented portrayal of reality generated by combining digital information with images of items seen through an electronic device, producing a participatory experience for the user (Zhu et al. [2019](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0139)). The results established that SLAM is appropriate for viewing three-dimensional furniture models on the intended surface of floor space without labels.

It has been demonstrated that an interconnected AR system may help a robot's operator carry out duties more efficiently and securely by utilizing computational modeling and digital twins Choi et al. ([2022](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0025)). By tracking the shared workspace and delivering user-centric visualization via smart, mixed reality, fully immersive glasses for safe and efficient human–robot collaboration, the system enables safe and efficient collaboration between humans and robots. The system creates a digital twin of the physical environment using 3D modeling and sensor data. One RGB-D sensor scans one region of the surroundings using 3D point cloud data, while another RGB-D sensor examines the opposite area while tracking the 3D skeletal data of the person. The two registered background scans are subjected to quick global registration, and the 3D point cloud of the target object is subjected to deep learning-based segmentation. Unlike the convex hull and voxelization method, this method enables real-time applicability without sacrificing the reliability of safety distance estimation for human–robot collaboration. A virtual model can be a successful risk assessment, safety planning, and education strategy (Pitkäaho et al. [2021](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0097)).

### 5.6 Safety Concerns of Reinforcement Learning

In educational robots or toy robots, where the environment in which they operate is predictable, a reinforcement learning agent is deployed (Sreenivas and Rao [2022](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0112)). However, in applications such as autonomous vehicles or industrial robots where operational conditions are not completely anticipated, the robot could enter an unfamiliar state as it has not been trained to handle such input (Gopinath et al. [2018](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0046), [2021](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0045)). A self-stabilization approach to tackling the safety challenges posed by reinforcement learning robots has been reported to make trained reinforcement learning robots quickly recover when perturbed (Gopinath et al. [2018](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0046), [2021](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0045)). Self-stabilization is a distributed software characteristic that enables a system to regain its original state despite the absence of centralized control (Chang et al. [2017](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0019); Luo et al. [2018](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0085)). Because the system can recover from unpredictable intermittent errors, it can be considered a specific instance of unmasking high availability.

García and Shafie ([2020](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0040)) suggested employing a secure learning algorithm to improve the gait of a robot with human features, allowing it to move faster with fewer accidents. The policy recycling for safe reinforcement learning algorithm addresses the safety concerns in reinforcement learning. During training, the policy recycling for safe reinforcement learning uses continuous risk functions and prior knowledge to identify potentially problematic circumstances. By implementing policy recycling, RNN-based controllers in robots can be configured to periodically reset their hidden states during training, decreasing error buildup and improving the stability and safety of the controller (He et al. [2021](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0055); Li et al. [2023](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0081); Wu et al. [2022](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0131)).

### 5.7 Safety Control System Verification and Validation

Input-based confirmation, systems analysis, and template methodologies are critical for evaluating robotic safety control systems. The safety of interaction between humans and robots in industrial processes has been studied and established by synthesizing control software. Still, it is challenging to guarantee the accuracy of safety regulators under specified conditions (Gleirscher et al. [2022](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0044)). The procedure, risk analysis, and appropriate safety regulations for the target purpose dictate the validation, testing, and certification process. It was proposed that using stochastic games to mimic all key environmental behaviors and unpredictable events raises the chance of safety and decreases the likelihood of mishaps (Gleirscher et al. [2022](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0044)).

Another study found that a method for robotized warehouses that uses hidden Markov model motion validation to measure human intent increases safety (Petković et al. [2019](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0096)). According to the output of the framework, base intentions can be effectively assessed within fair and reasonable limits. This method can be used to improve the efficacy of robot trajectory with many humans.

The process of safety certification for industrial robotic control systems was co-verified using a hardware/software co-verification strategy and an ABB industrial paint robot as a case study (Murray et al. [2022](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0092)). The paint robot uses a high-voltage electrostatic control system to ensure safe operation, while the hardware and software verification models are related via platform mappings. The platform mappings constitute a valuable and practical combination of highly specialized and diverse tools and techniques.

## 6 Ergonomics in Human–Robot Interactive Systems

In any functional ecosystem, particularly during the Fourth Industrial Revolution (4IR), the human–robot interaction approach has progressively adopted a human-centric design philosophy. The International Ergonomics Association defines ergonomically engineered systems as a scientific investigation of human interaction with other items, including robots, to enhance comfort and efficiency.

Ergonomics is a fundamental design element that underpins all technology systems, particularly those incorporating collaborative robotics. The importance of ergonomic design in HRI was emphasized by Benos et al. ([2020](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0009)), who investigated the ergonomic risks associated with cooperative agricultural robots. It was stressed that assessing workplace risk factors is critical for preventing musculoskeletal disorders and protecting against ergonomic hazards. This method is based on the concept that robots must be constructed to prevent physical risks such as pinch points and sharp edges, as well as minimize harmful vibrations and optimize control systems like gravity correction. These concerns contribute to a broader framework that promotes safety, well-being, and efficiency.

Figure [8](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-fig-0008) shows that the design of human–robot interaction systems has to achieve a delicate balance between safety, ergonomics, and operational efficiency (Gualtieri et al. [2022](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0049)). This includes lowering occupational dangers, improving operator well-being during robot contact, and reducing work time and expenses.
