Considerations when designing for human-centric industrial human–robot interaction (HRI) (Gualtieri et al. [2020](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0050)).

These principles in Table [3](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-tbl-0003 "Link to table") are part of a broader set of guidelines proposed to aid designers in creating collaborative assembly environments that are not only safe and ergonomic but also highly efficient (Gualtieri et al. [2021](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0048), [2022](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0049); Gualtieri et al. [2020](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0047); Gualtieri et al. [2020](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0050); Rojas et al. [2020](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0103)).

|  | Guidelines |
| --- | --- |
| Motion planning | - i. <br>  Refrain from using upper limbs for an extended period during assembly.<br>  <br>- ii. <br>  Avoid positioning elbows above shoulder level while assembling.<br>  <br>- iii. <br>  Avoid constant use of average force and force peaks during assembly.<br>  <br>- iv. <br>  Avoid continuous grasping using fingertips during assembly.<br>  <br>- v. <br>  Avoid frequent and repetitive movements of the upper limbs during assembly.<br>  <br>- vi. <br>  Employ smooth trajectories that imitate natural human arm movements.<br>  <br>- vii. <br>  Use swing trajectories instead of continuously straight trajectories.<br>  <br>- viii. <br>  Avoid high-speed motions.<br>  <br>- ix. <br>  Use motion planning that takes into account human factors. |
| Workstation elements | - x. <br>  Decrease the weight of heavy equipment and devices that operators manipulate.<br>  <br>- xi. <br>  Refrain from using workspaces that require keeping the workstation components away from the body during assembly.<br>  <br>- xii. <br>  Do not use workspaces that require vertical displacement of workstation elements beyond the range between hips and shoulders during assembly.<br>  <br>- xiii. <br>  Avoid workspaces that require frequent body movements to manage workstation elements during assembly.<br>  <br>- xiv. <br>  Highlight elements, make them visible, and provide visual or auditory feedback to help operators identify them.<br>  <br>- xv. <br>  Use tactile discrimination by size and texture to make elements distinguishable and offer visual or auditory feedback.<br>  <br>- xvi. <br>  Design the work intuitively to assist in forming a mental model, decrease reaction time, facilitate learning transfer, and promote similarity. |
| Robot system | - i. <br>  Employ rounded edges and corners and compliant and smooth surfaces to increase the contact surface area.<br>  <br>- ii. <br>  Manage energy absorption by using padding, cushioning, deformable components, or safety-rated soft axis to reduce impact forces or enlarge energy transfer time.<br>  <br>- iii. <br>  Implement restrictions on the moving masses.<br>  <br>- iv. <br>  Use mass and velocity to limit mechanical power, momentum, or energy.<br>  <br>- v. <br>  Design the end-effector to safeguard against hazards related to the workpiece.<br>  <br>- vi. <br>  Incorporate hardware to restrict the forces or torques of robot systems, including the end-effector. |
| Organizational measures | - i. <br>  Operators should be informed about the robot's speed.<br>  <br>- ii. <br>  Operators should be involved in defining the layout and work activities.<br>  <br>- iii. <br>  The work should be made intuitive to promote the formation of a mental model, reduce reaction time, facilitate learning transfer, and enhance similarity.<br>  <br>- iv. <br>  The motion of robot systems should be signaled.<br>  <br>- v. <br>  Keep an eye on robot systems performance.<br>  <br>- vi. <br>  The access routes (e.g., paths used by operators and materials to reach the collaborative workspace) should be established to prevent operator and robot misuse of production resources and enhance efficiency. |

The scope of ergonomic considerations goes beyond physical design. Collaborative robots must be accessible and intuitive, adaptable to different work environments, and mindful of legal and ethical responsibilities. They should also contribute to long-term sustainability and have a positive economic impact, both from a cost perspective and by increasing user satisfaction. For example, the physical design must prioritize user comfort, promote trust, and enhance the user experience. Robots may be employed by a wide range of people and industries because of intuitive interfaces, adaptive design aspects, and consideration for the larger workplace. Furthermore, bringing robots focused on the situations of the people necessitates a thorough understanding of legal safety standards and associated liability risks. Looking ahead, the design must include sustainability, with well-designed, ergonomic robots expected to have a longer service life, require less maintenance, and contribute to more responsible robotics deployment in industries such as manufacturing and healthcare.

Ergonomics in human–robot interacting systems is a broad and important area of study. It is about how technology interacts with humans and the development of systems in which robots are more than just tools but collaborate with humans. By incorporating ergonomic concepts into the design, development, and deployment of collaborative robots, the Fourth Industrial Revolution's promise of improving lives, work, and society in safe, comfortable, efficient, and inclusive ways can be fulfilled.

## 7 Safety Evaluation in Medical Robotics

In recent years, the field of medical robots has seen an unprecedented change in safety assessment and evaluation. Many ideas, procedures, and technology improvements have emerged, especially regarding robotic-assisted surgeries, showing significant promise for improving healthcare delivery and patient outcomes.

One of the most significant developments in medical robotics safety was the development of a system using LabVIEW that operates on a National Instruments CompactRIO platform. This innovative method facilitated fine-safety-related elements to influence robot-mediated therapy, indicating an important step toward programmable safety controls (Mancisidor et al. [2019](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0087)). Furthermore, the criticality of security within these advanced medical systems cannot be understated. Madden et al. ([2019](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0086)) led an in-depth investigation of safety measures, emphasizing the importance of strict controls and barriers to prevent unauthorized access to robotic systems, protecting both patients and care providers.

As medical robotic technologies advance, so do clinical applications. Research has revealed that robotic assistance can improve a wide range of surgical operations. Gynaecologic procedures have witnessed significant advancements in precision, resulting in fewer complications (Madden et al. [2019](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0086)). Furthermore, in treatments such as distal gastrectomy for stage IA gastric cancer and major hepatectomy, robotic-assisted surgeries have made significant improvements in patient satisfaction (Hosoda et al. [2019](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0059); Choi and Byeon [2020](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0024)). These advancements apply to several fields, including transoral robotic surgery for oral and laryngopharyngeal cancer, robotic-assisted cyst excision for choledochal cysts, and transthoracic robotic techniques in rib resection (Park et al. 2020; Yin et al. [2022](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0133); Burt et al. 2021).

Despite positive improvements, medical robotic safety remains a major concern. Robotic malfunctions are a major problem, particularly in gynecologic and colorectal situations (Tapper et al. [2019](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0118)). This circumstance highlights the critical need for collaboration among industry experts and clinicians to investigate the root cause of such malfunctions and develop improved solutions. Figure [9](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-fig-0009) depicts emergency safety protocols and training environments, emphasizing the importance of role clarity, process understanding, and risk mitigation (Kalipershad and Peristerakis [2022](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0071)).

[![Details are in the caption following the image](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/c2764746-0780-4ec5-b80b-582a9f579263/rob70022-fig-0009-m.png)](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/b4cf933f-b6bb-4dce-937e-cbca2787e064/rob70022-fig-0009-m.jpg)

**Figure 9**

[Open in figure viewer](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#) [PowerPoint](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/action/downloadFigures?id=rob70022-fig-0009&partId=&doi=10.1002%2Frob.70022)

Visual aid of the significant steps within the emergency safety protocol, adopted from (Kalipershad and Peristerakis [2022](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0071)).

While surgical robots can be considered a kind of flagship for medical robotics, other types of medical robots – nursing robots and noninvasive medical assistance robots – share many safety concerns. Nursing robots are developed to support routine work by health workers and thus deal with problems of equipment failure, interdisciplinary collaboration, and ethical concerns. Failures of the robots used in nursing, for example, may lead to cases of medication errors or delays in the care provided for patients, and emphasize the development and implementation of enhanced safety measures along with constant monitoring.

Safety issues also arise with noninvasive medical aid robots, which are very valuable in services such as patient monitoring and rehabilitation. Failures in such robots result in inadequate patient support or inaccurate data interpretation, which might compromise the health of the patient. Furthermore, for these robots to integrate seamlessly into healthcare, effective coordination is required to ensure smooth operation with minimal hazards.

Looking to the future, medical robots have enormous potential. However, its full understanding necessitates persistent collaboration among stakeholders like policymakers, medical practitioners, academicians, engineers, and researchers. This joint effort will facilitate regulatory compliance, addressing issues related to ethical practices, including patient consent and data protection, and constant R&D. Furthermore, the regulatory bodies must align with R&D output and technology advancement to enhance the safe integration of robotic systems and applications into the medical sectors. More importantly, ethical concerns –consent, autonomy, and privacy – require extraordinary consideration and background knowledge within the framework of robotic-assisted surgeries. Equipment development through interdisciplinary collaboration would certainly enhance patient safety, and this is achievable through continual improvement, meticulous research, and training.

## 8 Safety Evaluation in Agricultural Robotics

This section discusses the recent advancements and safety considerations in the deployment of robots in agricultural sectors.

The application of robots in agricultural sectors has increased recently, particularly in modern and mechanized farming. These include operations like monitoring, weeding, planting, and harvesting. The application of robotics in agricultural operations aims to minimize labor costs and increase crop/food production and overall efficiency. However, the application of robots in agricultural operations presents safety issues due to the dynamic nature of farming operations and environments.

### 8.1 Unique Safety Challenges in Agricultural Robotics

- i.
Dynamic and unstructured environments: Farmlands are nonuniform topography – varying crop heights, rocks trees, plains, and valleys – and unstable weather conditions, making the agricultural environment different from controlled industrial settings. Therefore, robots must safely navigate the agricultural environment characterized by this unpredictability (Duckett et al. [2018](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0034); Ramin Shamshiri et al. [2018](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0099)).

- ii.
Human–animal interaction: Agricultural robots often operate together with livestock and farm workers. Thus, ensuring human and animal safety can be achieved through advanced detection and avoidance systems (Bechar and Vigneault. [2017](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0008)).

- iii.
Use of harmful substances, equipment, and tools: agricultural operations like the use of heavy-duty machinery and spraying pesticides cause environmental pollution and injury if not properly managed (Kaur et al. [2024](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0072); Oude Elferink et al. [2019](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0095)).


Several studies have been conducted on the practical applications of robotic safety assessment in the agricultural sector. For instance, autonomous weeding robots like “Ladybird” were developed to detect obstacles and prevent the destruction of crops while guaranteeing the safety of the farm workers (Gil et al. [2023](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0043); Zhang et al. [2022](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0135)). Similarly, fruit-picking robots employ force sensors and soft grippers to handle fragile produce and vision systems to detect the presence of humans in the surroundings (Dischinger et al. [2021](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0032); Visentin et al. [2023](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0122)). Drones with geofencing and obstacle avoidance features ensure human safety during farm operations including pesticide application and crop monitoring (Ahmed et al. [2021](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0003); Ingole et al. [2024](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0062)).

It is important to talk about the popular international standard in robotic safety, specifically in agricultural robotics – ISO 18497:2018. The ISO 18497:2018 standard specifies the safety guidelines for extremely automated agricultural machines, and the standard encompasses robot performance evaluation and functional safety ISO (International Organization for Standardization) ([2018](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0063)). National and regional regulatory organizations are making policies to control the use of autonomous machines in the agricultural sector, highlighting environmental concerns and safety considerations (Shockley et al. [2022](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0109); Shutske et al. [2023](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0110)).

### 8.2 Recent Safety Strategies and Technologies

Many of the recent studies on agricultural robotics have focused on developing solutions and strategies for safety improvement and risk management. A few examples include the following:



- i.
Advanced sensing and perception: A combination of cameras, radar, LiDAR, and sensor fusion techniques supports real-time detection of humans, obstacles, and animals on the farm, augmenting safe navigation (Korthals et al. [2018](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0074); Reina et al. [2016](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0101)).

- ii.
Autonomous navigation and control: the implementation of cutting-edge algorithms for path prediction and control helps robots to become familiar with dynamic environments associated with the farming environment (Wahdan [2021](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0125)).

- iii.
Robot–human interaction protocols: The development of detailed communication methods between operators and robots improves situational consciousness and safety (Coronado et al. [2022](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0026); Rodriguez-Guerra et al. [2021](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0102)).


Future research should concentrate on creating systems that facilitate effortless and safe interaction between robots and humans in shared agricultural tasks to further enhance safety in the agricultural sector. Due to the dynamic conditions associated with the farming environment, designing robots that can withstand harsh environmental conditions while maintaining safety features is also important.

Safety evaluation in agricultural robots is necessary to successfully incorporate these technologies into farming methods. Agricultural robots must be designed to tackle the specific problems of agricultural contexts and equipped with advanced safety features to ensure the safety of humans, animals, and the environment.

## 9 Recommendations for Future Work

While substantial strides have been made in ensuring safety in human–robot interaction, as detailed in this review, some areas still require further research to guarantee the safety of the people.

### 9.1 Extending Safety Approaches to Multi-Human and Multi-Robot Systems

While most industrial environments require more than one human and robot working together, these interactions normally are modeled between a single human and another robot. As explained earlier in Section [5.3](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-sec-0320), multi-robot collaboration collision, the implementation of dynamic controllers, and recurrent neural networks have shown promise in preventing collisions among multiple robots (He et al. [2021](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0055)). Similarly, in the future, such methods should also be extended to complex scenarios of multiple humans and robots to increase coherence with the complexity, since in interactions, different complexities of the systems bring higher risks for potential accidents.

### 9.2 Improving Risk Assessment Procedures

It is challenging to ensure safety in robotics, and this is because it is hard to model all relevant characteristics of dynamic environments and uncontrollable actions. As emphasized in Section [4](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-sec-0070) on Risk Assessment, state-of-the-art approaches – HAZOP-UML and HRN methods – do not capture risks associated with advanced robotic systems that come with many nuances. The reasons for the failure of the current risk appraisal categories are that they are adapted to conventional automation systems and not for complex robotic systems, given that there are diverse inputs from those systems affecting behavior. Therefore, the need to develop comprehensive and adaptive frameworks for risk assessment that can effectively capture these factors is recommended for further study.

### 9.3 Exploring Safety Frameworks Beyond Human–Robot Collaboration

Few research papers consider the safety frameworks of robotic systems outside human–robot interactive systems. Robotics technology has gradually expanded into applications involving robot swarms, articulated robots, and automated guided vehicles. This is evidenced in Section [5.2](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-sec-0230) on Safety in Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM), which shows how SLAM algorithms are increasingly found in applications in autonomous navigation for both AGVs and AMRs (Mur-Artal and Tardos [2017](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0091)). However, most of the multi-robot systems have not considered their safety implications. Future research should look at unique safety challenges posed by these systems and develop appropriate safety models and control strategies for more complex robotic ecosystems.

### 9.4 Investigating Comprehensive Safety Regulations and Policies

Though Section [3](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-sec-0060) (regulatory standards in robotics safety) outlines the existing standards, such as ISO 10218 and ISO 15066, these do not fully give solutions to the safety concerns brought about by the integration of robots into complex work environments. Research efforts should focus on developing comprehensive robot safety regulations, protocols, and policies that prioritize human safety, safeguard work equipment, and ensure a secure workplace and environment. This holistic approach is necessary given the broader implications of robotic deployments, including environmental hazards and equipment interactions, areas where current standards have proven insufficient.

### 9.5 Enhancing Ergonomic Safety in Robotic Design

As discussed in Section 6 on Ergonomics in Human–Robot Interactive Systems, ergonomic considerations are important to ensure operator well-being and efficiency. Even with the guidelines provided in the following table, there is still a great deal of study needed to establish ergonomic safety considerations in designing robotic systems that take into consideration wearable robots specifically because of their interaction directly with the human body. In this respect, research work should be directed at reducing musculoskeletal strain and ensuring comfort during prolonged use. There is also a deficiency in integrating ergonomic principles deeply enough into the design process concerning injury prevention and improving user acceptance.

### 9.6 Understanding Risk Analysis in Smart Application Systems

New challenges of interconnectivity and interoperability occur through the Internet of Things and the advances within smart application systems. As highlighted in Section 5.5, Safety Applications of Augmented Reality, Virtual Reality, and Mixed Reality, their combined adoption improves safety but also introduces new hazards regarding system interconnectivity (Choi et al. [2022](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0025)). Further research to gain a full understanding of risk analysis and the monitoring of interconnectivity in such smart systems is recommended. This will call for methodologies relating to the detection and mitigation of cybersecurity threats, system failures, and unplanned interactions by interconnected devices.

### 9.7 Incorporating Safety Assurance Into Robotic Design

Safety assurance in every layer of robotics design, from the structural design of the robot to mechanical components, motion control algorithms, and overall architecture, is necessary to achieve many layers of safety. The employment of strict verification and validation methods, for example, hardware/software co-verification, ensures the functionality of the safety mechanisms under any condition specified (Murray et al. [2022](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rob.70022?af=R#rob70022-bib-0092)). Embedded safety allows the robot to be better positioned for much better reliability and fewer failures that can, in turn, lead to accidents.

## 10 Conclusion

This study looked into many safety issues that should be considered while designing and deploying robotic systems. Compared to other industrial setups, fewer works of literature present the safety considerations and risk assessment in the deployment of robots. Safety in industries where robotics is used is critical as there is a need for testing, operation, and maintenance. Risky actions or conditions can lead to injuries, equipment damage, environmental damage, or financial loss.

Risk assessment in the industry involves identifying a machine's operational limitations, detecting potential hazards to machine operators, evaluating these risks, and determining their consequences. A computational risk assessment technique was developed to reduce the impact of subjective human judgment. This technique utilizes a uniform rule integrating information on processes, capabilities, and threats. Mathematical formalists with roots in system analysis have been used to assess collaborative robots’ technological and social actions. This information theory-driven process analysis uses multilevel frameworks to explore concerns step by step methodically.

Research indicates that industrial robotic accidents frequently happen from judgment errors and risk evaluation lapses by operators. The Risk Ranking Number (HRN) has been designed to quantify the safety evaluation process and define intervals. The HRN calculation has been reported to be based on four principal variables: LO (Likelihood of Occurrence), FE (Frequency of Exposure), DPH (Degree of Possible Harm), and NP (Number of Persons at Risk). However, DPH is regarded as the most critical, signifying the gravity of possible risks.

Emphasis has been placed on the importance of analyzing job risk factors to avoid musculoskeletal disorders and deal with ergonomic hazards. This idea is based on the fact that when designing robots, one of the most important things to consider is the elimination of physical dangers like sharp edges and pinch points. The reduction of harmful vibrations and making control systems like gravity compensation are practices that improve the safety ratings of robotic design. When making human–robot interaction systems, designers must find the right balance between safety, ergonomics, and the system's functionality. By incorporating ergonomic principles into the design, development, and deployment of collaborative robots, the promise of the Fourth Industrial Revolution can be realized: a transformation that enhances the quality of life, work environments, and societal inclusivity through safe, efficient, and comfortable means.

This study should create awareness and assist designers, engineers, implementors, operators, or users in decisively demonstrating the safety assurance of robotic systems.

web.title: Robot Programming Best Practices for Safety and Reliability
web.description: Learn best practices for robot programming, from homing sequences and modular program structure to verification, with a pick-and-place walkthrough.
web.markdown:
[Skip to content](https://intelligentintegrators.org/robot-programming-best-practices/#content)

Effective robot programming is the backbone of any successful automation system. When programming [industrial robots](https://intelligentintegrators.org/industrial-robot-guide/ ""), the quality of your program directly determines the safety of your operators, the longevity of your equipment, and the reliability of your production output. A poorly structured program is difficult to read, painful to troubleshoot, and potentially dangerous. A well-structured one, on the other hand, can be maintained and scaled with confidence.

Over years of hands-on experience programming cartesian (sometimes referred to as five-axis) robots, six-axis, and [collaborative robots](https://intelligentintegrators.org/collaborative-robots-industrial-automation/ ""), I have developed a consistent methodology that I apply regardless of the robot type or application. At a high level, it comes down to three pillars:

1. Building the homing sequence
2. Structuring the program using a main program and subprograms
3. Verifying the program through rigorous testing

This article walks through each of these pillars in detail, using a pick-and-place robot program as a practical example along the way.

## The Homing Sequence

#### **Why Begin Robot Programming with the Homing Sequence?**

It might seem counterintuitive to build the homing sequence before anything else, but there is a practical reason: the process of designing the home return forces you to think through the physical layout of your robot cell. You begin to understand the obstacles and devices the robot arm must navigate around, and that situational awareness becomes invaluable as you build out the rest of your program. Additionally, once your homing routine is established, you can use it to safely restart and reposition the robot as you iterate on the main program and subprograms. It makes the entire development process smoother.

#### The Goal: Hands-Off Operation

One of the most important principles to embed into any homing sequence is ease of use for the operator. The ideal homing routine requires nothing more than the press of a single button. Every step that requires the operator to manually jog the robot, interact with auxiliary equipment, or make a judgment call introduces risk, both to the operator and to the equipment itself.

By designing the homing sequence so that the robot program controls all movements, inputs, and outputs autonomously, you reduce operator liability and minimize the margin for error. This increases program complexity on the development side, but that tradeoff is well worth it in a production environment.

#### A Stepped, Position-Aware Approach to Robot Programming

The most critical aspect of programming a home return is understanding where the robot is before you move it. Never assume the robot is in a known position when the homing sequence is triggered. It could be mid-cycle, partially inside a fixture, or positioned near an auxiliary device that is still in its working state.

The approach I use is a **stepped, position-aware method**. Before commanding any movement, the program checks the robot’s current position, either by reading positional data directly or by monitoring status bits that are toggled throughout the program’s execution cycle. Based on that information, the program determines what the robot can safely do to begin clearing its current location and navigating back to the designated home position.

For example, using a cartesian coordinate reference frame: if there is a device located 500 millimeters from the robot’s origin in the X direction, the first step of the homing sequence should check whether the robot’s X position is safe relative to that device. Is the effector already clear? If not, what is the safest direction to move first? The answers to those questions drive the logic of your stepped homing routine.

#### **Monitoring I/O and Auxiliary Device States**

Physical movement is only half of the homing challenge. The other half is managing the state of all inputs and outputs, your own robot’s end-of-arm tooling, as well as every auxiliary device in the work cell.

Consider a scenario where the robot arm is holding a work piece inside a stamping or drilling device. Before the robot can move, the program must confirm that the auxiliary device has fully retracted and released the work piece. Moving the arm while a device is still in its working position can cause catastrophic equipment damage.

Your homing sequence should include logic that actively monitors the status of each relevant device and only proceeds with robot movement once all devices are confirmed to be in a safe state. This is not optional, it is a fundamental safety requirement.

#### **Homing Sequence Checklist**

To summarize, a well-designed homing sequence should:

Require minimal operator intervention, ideally a single button press

Check the robot’s current position before initiating any movement

Take a stepped approach, clearing one device or zone at a time

Monitor the state of all relevant auxiliary devices and I/O before moving

Protect the safety of operators above all else, and equipment second

## **The Main Program and Subprograms**

#### **Why Modular Structure Matters in Robot Programming**

Once the homing sequence is in place, it is time to build the main program. The philosophy here centers on readability and maintainability. A robot program that consists of thousands of lines in a single block is extraordinarily difficult to troubleshoot, especially for someone who did not write it. Modular programming, organizing logic into a main program that calls discrete subprograms, solves this problem.

The main program serves as the high-level map of how the robot executes. Anyone reading it should be able to understand the overall flow of operation at a glance, without needing to parse through low-level position data or I/O commands. Those details live in the subprograms.

Note that depending on the robot controller and software you are working with, some platforms already have a designated main program structure. Others, particularly older controllers, require you to create this architecture manually. Either way, the principle remains the same.

#### Structure of the Main Program

Defining the intended application is a critical step in selecting a robot. Different tasks, such as welding, assembly, material handling, or inspection, require specific motion profiles, payload capacities, and control precision.

The working environment is also an important consideration. Robots operating near humans may need collaborative features, while SCARA and Delta robots are often better suited for high-speed tasks or space-constrained cells.

By evaluating operational requirements like cycle time, path accuracy, and environmental conditions, engineers can identify the most suitable robot configuration. Choosing a system with flexible programming and adaptable tooling further ensures the robot can accommodate process changes and future applications.

#### **The Main Program and Subprograms**

At its core, the main program should contain the following:

- An initial call to the homing sequence (or a trigger that initiates it based on operator input)
- A set of conditions that must be met before each subprogram is called
- Calls to the relevant subprograms in the correct sequence
- A loop or jump command that returns the program to its starting point after a completed cycle

The conditions that gate each subprogram call are just as important as the subprograms themselves. These conditions serve as a safety net, ensuring that each phase of the robot’s operation only begins when the environment is ready for it.

## Applying Robot Programming Best Practices to a Pick-and-Place Program

A pick-and-place application is one of the most common robot tasks and serves as an excellent example of this structure in action. In this scenario, the robot picks a part from one location and places it at another. The main program might look like this conceptually:

1. Execute homing sequence
2. Check conditions for picking → call Pick subroutine
3. Check conditions for placing → call Place subroutine
4. Return to step 2 and repeat

That is the entire main program. It’s clear, readable, and easy to follow. The complexity lives inside the subroutines, where it belongs.

#### The Place Subroutine

The Place subroutine follows a similar logic pattern, but the conditions focus on the destination rather than the source. Before moving the robot arm toward the drop-off zone, the program must verify that the area is clear. A part-present sensor at the destination serves this purpose: if a part is already there, the robot waits. Only when the zone is confirmed clear does the arm move in.

Once the robot reaches the target position, the gripper opens and releases the part. The arm then retracts to a safe intermediate position and returns to its ready state, waiting for the next cycle to begin.

#### Notes on Subprogram Design

A few principles to keep in mind when building subprograms:

- **Redundancy is acceptable.** Issuing a command that may already be in effect (like opening an already-open gripper) helps ensure known states and does not harm the program.
- **Condition checking before movement is non-negotiable.** Never move the robot arm into a zone without first confirming that zone is safe to enter.
- **Sensor feedback matters at every stage.** Detecting part presence on the gripper, at the pick station, and at the place station all contribute to a robust, fault-tolerant program.

## Verifying Your Robot Programming

#### **The Most Important Step**

Of the three pillars described in this article, verification is arguably the most critical. A homing sequence can be elegant, and a main program can be beautifully structured, but neither of those things matter if the program has not been rigorously tested against real-world conditions before it goes into production.

Verification means deliberately thinking through every scenario your robot might encounter and confirming that the program handles each one safely and correctly. This is not a step to rush.

#### Robot Programming Test Scenarios to Cover

The starting point for verification is any scenario that could potentially cause harm, to an operator, or to the equipment. Ask yourself:

- What happens if a sensor fails or provides an unexpected reading?
- What happens if the gripper misses the part?
- What happens if an auxiliary device does not complete its cycle?
- What happens if the homing sequence is triggered at an unusual point in the robot’s cycle?
- What happens if the robot loses power mid-cycle and is restarted?

Each of these scenarios should be tested, or at minimum simulated as closely as possible, before the program is deployed in a live environment. The goal is to leave no plausible failure mode untested.

#### Safety First, Equipment Second

It bears repeating: the purpose of verification is, first and foremost, to protect people. Operator safety is the top priority. Equipment protection is a close second. Any test scenario that involves risk to a person should be conducted with all appropriate [safety protocols](https://intelligentintegrators.org/industrial-robot-safety-standards/ "") in place, reduced speed, e-stop within reach, and no personnel inside the robot’s working envelope unless the situation specifically requires it.

## Conclusion

Effective robot programming is not just about knowing how to write code for a specific controller. It is about developing a methodology that produces programs that are safe, readable, maintainable, and reliable. The three-pillar approach described in this article, homing sequence, modular program structure, and verification, provides exactly that kind of framework.

Here are the most critical takeaways:

#### **The Homing Sequence**

- Build it first; it shapes everything that follows.
- Design for minimal operator intervention, the best homing routines require a single button press.
- Always check the robot’s position and the state of all auxiliary devices before initiating any movement.

#### **Program Structure**

- Use a main program to define high-level flow, and subprograms for detailed logic.
- Gate every subprogram call with appropriate conditions; never move the robot into a zone without confirming it is safe.
- A modular structure is not just good practice, it is what makes programs readable and easy to troubleshoot by others.

#### **Verification**

- This step is the most important of the three.
- Think through every failure scenario, especially those involving risk to operators.
- Test deliberately and thoroughly before going live.

Robot programming, done well, is a discipline of structured thinking as much as it is a technical skill. Apply these principles consistently, and you will build programs that perform reliably and stand the test of time.

_This article is for educational purposes only and is not a certified training document. Intelligent Integrators is not responsible for damage resulting from or misuse of guides. Refer to the Terms and Conditions Page for more information._

Michael is the Founder & Content Manager of Intelligent Integrators. His experience comes from over 3 years as a Manufacturing Engineer in the injection molding industry. Specifically, he has led projects in robot programming, PLC programming, and electromechanical design.

web.title: What need to be cautioned when use robots? |robot arm safety
web.description: Part 2: The Golden Rules of Robot Arm Safety · Rule 1: Never Assume a Robot is Powered Down. Always treat a robot as if it is live and active.
web.markdown:
![robot arm safety](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20400%20300'%3E%3C/svg%3E)

- September 2, 2025
- pruce
- [Blog](https://www.hitbotrobot.com/hitbot/blog/)

- 0


### The Essential Guide to Robot Arm Safety: Rules, Risks, and Best Practices

Welcome to the era of automation! Robot arms are transforming industries, from manufacturing and logistics to healthcare and agriculture. These powerful machines enhance productivity, improve precision, and handle tasks that are dangerous or repetitive for humans. However, with great power comes great responsibility. A robot arm, while incredibly useful, is also a powerful piece of industrial machinery that demands respect and careful handling.

Ignoring safety protocols can lead to severe injuries, costly damage, and significant downtime. Whether you’re a seasoned engineer or new to automation, understanding and implementing robust robot arm safety guidance is non-negotiable. This comprehensive guide will walk you through the potential risks, fundamental rules, and crucial safety measures you need to know when integrating and operating a robot arm.

#### Part 1: Understanding the [Risks](https://safesitehq.com/industrial-robotics/) – Why Safety is Paramount

Before diving into the rules, it’s vital to understand what you’re safeguarding against. The risks associated with robot arms are multifaceted and often underestimated.

1.  Impact and Collision Hazards: This is the most immediate risk. A robot arm moves with significant force and speed. A worker caught in its path or struck by the arm or its payload can suffer crushing injuries, fractures, or lacerations. These incidents often occur during:

- Teach Mode: When a programmer is within the robot’s work envelope to set points or test paths.
- Unpredicted Movements: Due to software errors, control system failures, or incorrect commands.
- Payload Ejection: If a gripper fails or a part is not secured properly, it can be thrown from the cell at high velocity.

2.  Pinch and Crush Points: The joints and moving linkages of a robot arm create numerous pinch points where a hand or finger can become trapped.
3.  Electrical Hazards: Robot systems operate on high voltage. Faulty wiring, damaged insulation, or improper lockout/tagout procedures during maintenance can lead to electrocution or electrical fires.
4.  Ergonomic Hazards: While robots are designed to reduce human strain, the tasks around them—such as manual loading/unloading outside the cell or repetitive maintenance—can create new ergonomic risks.
5.  System and Programming Errors: Bugs in the code, incorrect calibration, or a corrupted program can cause the robot to behave unpredictably, moving to unexpected positions or ignoring its safety limits.
6.  Environmental Hazards: The robot’s application can introduce additional risks, such as exposure to lasers in welding robots, extreme heat in foundry applications, or biohazards in medical labs.

The key takeaway is that robot accidents are rarely due to the robot “malfunctioning” on its own. They are most often the result of human error, a lack of training, or a failure in the surrounding safety systems.

![robot arm safety](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20680%20388'%3E%3C/svg%3E)

#### Part 2: The Golden Rules of [Robot Arm Safety](https://www.hitbotrobot.com/faq-robotics-arm/)

These foundational principles should form the bedrock of your safety culture.

- Rule 1: Never Assume a Robot is Powered Down. Always treat a robot as if it is live and active. The only way to be sure it is safe to enter its work range is to follow a strict Lockout/Tagout (LOTO) procedure.
- Rule 2: Know the Work Range Intimately. The work range (or operating zone) is the 3D space within which the robot can move. Clearly mark this area on the floor and never enter it while the robot is operational unless specific safety measures are in place and active (e.g., in safeguarded space with reduced speed).
- Rule 3: Prioritize Training Above All. Everyone who interacts with the robot—programmers, operators, maintenance staff, and even personnel who simply work near it—must receive thorough, role-specific safety training. This is not a one-time event but an ongoing process.
- Rule 4: Respect the Safety Systems. Physical guards, light curtains, and emergency stops are not suggestions; they are critical, life-saving devices. Never bypass them or remove guards for “convenience.”
- Rule 5: Develop and Follow Detailed Procedures. Documented procedures for operation, maintenance, teaching, and emergency response ensure consistency and safety. Everyone must know what to do and when to do it.

#### Part 3: A Practical Safety Guidance Framework

Implementing safety is a step-by-step process. Here’s a framework covering the entire lifecycle of the robot arm.

1. Pre-Installation: The Safety-by-Design Phase
2.  Risk Assessment: Before the robot even arrives, conduct a formal risk assessment. Identify all potential hazards for every task and interaction point.
3.  Cell Design and Safeguarding: Design the robotic cell with safety in mind. This includes:

- Physical Barriers: Fixed fencing, gates with safety interlocks (the robot stops if a gate is opened).
- Presence-Sensing Safeguards: Install light curtains, laser scanners, or pressure-sensitive mats. These can either halt the robot or trigger a reduced-speed         operating mode when a person is detected.
- Clear Markings: Clearly mark the work envelope, hazard zones, and safe pathways for personnel.

1. Operational Safety: Day-to-Day Vigilance
2.  Emergency Stops (E-Stops): Ensure bright red E-Stop buttons are easily accessible on all sides of the cell and on teach pendants. Test them regularly.
3.  The Teach Pendant: This is a primary source of risk.

Mastering Control: Always retain sole control of the teach pendant. Use a physical key switch to toggle between modes (Auto/Manual).

Enable Device: Use a three-position deadman switch. The robot only operates when the switch is in the middle “enabled” position. Releasing or pressing it too         hard will cause the robot to stop.

Reduced Speed: Teaching must only be done in “Teach Mode,” which automatically limits the robot’s speed to a safe level (e.g., 250 mm/s or less).

3.  Personal Protective Equipment (PPE): Mandate appropriate PPE, such as safety glasses, steel-toed boots, and gloves, for anyone near the operational cell.
4. Maintenance and Troubleshooting
5.  Lockout/Tagout (LOTO): This is the most critical procedure. Before any maintenance, the power source must be isolated, locked with a personal lock, and tagged to inform others that work is being done.
6.  Software Safety: Implement password protection for different user levels to prevent unauthorized changes to programs or safety parameters. Regularly back up programs and safety configurations.
7. Collaborative Robot (Cobot) Specifics

While cobots are designed to work alongside humans with built-in force-limiting and collision detection, they are not inherently safe. Their safety is application-dependent.

Risk Assessment is Still Mandatory: A cobot lifting a heavy, sharp object poses a much greater risk than one handling a foam block.

Pay Attention to Payload and Tooling: The weight and shape of the end-of-arm tooling (e.g., a sharp gripper) must be considered in the risk assessment.

Monitor Speed and Force: Even with built-in sensors, configure the cobot’s speed and force limits appropriately for its specific shared task.

#### Part 4: Building a Culture of Safety

Technology alone cannot prevent accidents. Safety is a culture that requires commitment from everyone, from management to the shop floor.

- Leadership Commitment: Management must prioritize and fund safety measures without compromise.
- Continuous Training: Hold regular safety meetings, refresher courses, and drills for emergency scenarios.
- Open Communication: Encourage employees to report near-misses, potential hazards, and suggestions for improvement without fear of reprisal. A near-miss is a free lesson preventing a future accident.

#### Conclusion: Safety is Your Most Valuable Feature

A robot arm is a tremendous asset, but its value is entirely dependent on its safe operation. By understanding the risks, adhering to the golden rules, and implementing a rigorous safety framework, you protect your most valuable resources: your people and your productivity. At HITBOT, we engineer safety into our robots from the ground up. However, we know that true safety for robot arm is achieved through the partnership of reliable technology and informed, vigilant users. We are committed to providing not only advanced robotic solutions but also the comprehensive support and documentation needed to operate them safely and efficiently.

Your safety journey starts here. For more detailed safety information specific to your HITBOT model, always refer to the official user manual. Have questions? Our technical support team is ready to help you build a safer, more productive workplace.Ready to automate with confidence? Contact HITBOT today to learn about our safe and reliable robotic solutions.

# Understanding robot joint limits in simulation

JUN 26, 2025 \|

Understanding robot joint limits in simulation is crucial for developing effective and efficient robotic systems. In this blog, we'll explore what robot joint limits are, why they are important in simulations, and how they can be effectively managed to ensure optimal performance and safety in robotic applications.

Understanding Robot Joint Limits

Robot joint limits refer to the physical constraints on the range of motion for each joint in a robotic arm or leg. These limits are defined by mechanical design, material properties, and safety considerations. For example, a robotic arm may have rotational joints that can only turn within a specified angle to prevent damage or collision with other parts of the robot or its environment.

In simulations, these joint limits are critical as they help to create a realistic model of how a robot can move. By accurately representing joint constraints, simulations can provide insights into how a robot will behave in real-world scenarios, allowing for better design and programming of robotic systems.

The Importance of Joint Limits in Simulation

Joint limits play a significant role in ensuring that simulations are both accurate and reliable. They serve several vital functions:

1\. \*\*Preventing Infeasible Movements\*\*: By respecting joint limits, simulations can prevent robots from attempting movements that are physically impossible or could lead to mechanical failure.

2\. \*\*Ensuring Safety\*\*: Adhering to joint limits helps prevent simulations from producing scenarios where the robot could harm itself, its surroundings, or humans working alongside it.

3\. \*\*Enhancing Realism\*\*: Accurate joint limits contribute to the realism of the simulation, providing more reliable data that can be used to predict the performance and behavior of the robot in various environments.

4\. \*\*Facilitating Better Control Algorithms\*\*: Understanding joint limits helps in designing control algorithms that ensure smooth and efficient robot movement while avoiding potential singularities or deadlocks.

Managing Joint Limits in Simulation

Effectively managing joint limits in simulations involves several strategies and considerations:

1\. \*\*Incorporating Accurate Models\*\*: Use precise models that reflect the physical constraints of the robot accurately. This involves detailed measurements and understanding of the robot's mechanical design.

2\. \*\*Implementing Constraint Handling Techniques\*\*: Employ algorithms that can handle constraints efficiently, such as constraint-based optimization techniques, to ensure that the robot's movements stay within the defined limits.

3\. \*\*Testing in Virtual Environments\*\*: Conduct extensive testing in virtual environments to explore the behavior of robots under various conditions, allowing for the identification and mitigation of potential issues related to joint limits.

4\. \*\*Calibration and Validation\*\*: Regularly calibrate the simulation models against real-world data to ensure that the joint limits and other parameters remain accurate and reflective of the actual robot's capabilities.

Challenges and Considerations

While managing joint limits in simulations offers numerous benefits, it also presents challenges. One such challenge is the trade-off between accuracy and computational efficiency. Highly detailed models that capture every aspect of joint limits can be computationally expensive, leading to slower simulations. Therefore, it's crucial to balance the level of detail with the available computational resources.

Another consideration is the dynamic nature of some tasks, which may require adaptive joint limits. For instance, wear and tear or changes in the robot's operational environment might necessitate updates to the joint limits over time.

Conclusion

In conclusion, understanding and managing robot joint limits in simulations is essential for creating realistic, safe, and efficient robotic systems. By accurately modeling joint constraints, employing advanced constraint-handling techniques, and conducting thorough testing, developers can ensure that their simulations provide valuable insights into the performance and behavior of robotic systems in the real world. As robotics technology continues to evolve, the importance of precise and reliable joint limit management in simulations will only grow, paving the way for more advanced and capable robotic applications.

**Dieser Artikel auf einen Blick:**

The programming of robots enables efficient and safe automation in industry, logistics and service. The article explains basics such as teach-in and offline programming, presents newer approaches such as no-code platforms and automatic programming, and discusses security guidelines. Practical examples show the economic significance for medium-sized companies. As a vendor-independent partner, EF Robotics supports you from analysis to integration and service.

## Challenges and opportunities in robot programming

[Industrial and service robotics](https://www.ef-robotics.de/lieferroboter) We are currently facing two key challenges: **Shortage of skilled workers** and increasing demands for efficiency and flexibility. Many managing directors and plant managers are faced with the task of automating their production without jeopardizing ongoing operations and without access to a large IT team. The classic programming of [robots](https://www.ef-robotics.de/warum-roboter) For a long time, it was considered complex and time-consuming. Each customization project required deep code knowledge — a hurdle that prevented many companies from exploiting the full potential of modern robotics. At the same time, **safety requirements** strict: European standards such as EN ISO 3691‑4 and EN ISO 13849‑1 define strict rules for driverless transport systems (AGVs) and their control systems. For decision makers, this means that an economic solution must ensure compliance with these standards in addition to process optimization.

## Core definitions and basics of programming

### What is robot programming?

Under [**robot programming**](https://automationspraxis.industrie.de/robotik/roboter-programmieren-grundlagen-und-trends-der-roboterprogrammierung/) This means defining movement sequences, sensor data and control logic so that a robot safely performs a desired task. There are two basic categories:

- **Online programming (teach‑in)**: The programmer uses the physical robot. It guides the arm to the desired positions manually or via a teach pendant; the system stores the coordinates. These methods include the classic _Teach-in_ procedures as well as playback approaches and direct parameter input via control panels. Observing the robot movement makes it easy to get started and requires only short training. As a result, one also speaks of _Teach robots_.
- **offline programming (OLP)**: Here, the program is created externally on the computer. CAD data from the cell is loaded into simulation software, movements and processes are virtually tested, optimized and only then transferred to the robot. This eliminates downtime in production. The OLP enables deep interventions in the control system and the exchange or optimization of source code.

EF Robotics im ZDF Morgenmagazin! #zdf #internorga #robotik - YouTube

Tap to unmute

[EF Robotics im ZDF Morgenmagazin! #zdf #internorga #robotik](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=O89fmVZZqu4) [EF Robotics](https://www.youtube.com/channel/UC_04uCeFssL12MJWAcO7GZA)

EF Robotics265 subscribers

### Robot programming methods

- **Teach-in**: The operator guides the robot arm manually or pendulum, and the software stores the positions. For easy handling in the service area, the movement pattern is repeated later. Advantage: low training costs. Disadvantage: time-consuming and inflexible, as every change to the physical robot must be learned.
- **Playback**: The robot is moved directly or via a model and then reproduces exactly this movement. Tremors or inaccuracies are interpolated by the control software.
- **Parameter programming**: Parametric inputs via PC or control panel define joint movements. This method is suitable for clearly structured tasks.
- **offline simulation**: 3D simulations make it possible to plan complex movements, identify collisions, and optimize paths. Digital twins connect the virtual model with the real cell through calibration.
- **Automatic programming**: Modern software automatically generates programs based on defined quality guidelines and CAD data. Studies show that OLP saves up to 80% of programming time and significantly reduces downtime. With automated OLP, employees without programming experience can create programs.
- **No‑code and visual programming**: Drag-and-drop interfaces allow you to create motion flows without code. Users configure blocks that assign actions to the robot. These approaches reduce learning costs and are ideal for collaborative and service robots.

### AGV vs. AMR

Driverless transport systems are divided into AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles) and AMRs (Autonomous Mobile Robots). **AGVs** travel on fixed routes, which are often specified by guidewires, magnetic strips or QR codes. They have low self-intelligence and stop when there are obstacles. **AMRs** On the other hand, use lasers, cameras and AI algorithms for free navigation. They create maps of the environment, recognize obstacles and navigate autonomously. This makes them suitable for dynamic layouts and can be quickly adapted without expensive infrastructure changes. Decision-makers should choose the technology that suits their own process stability: stable, linear processes benefit from AGVs; diverse intralogistics or catering from AMRs.

## Benefits and benefits for decision makers

For managing directors and production managers, the **economic efficiency** of a robotics project. Choosing the right programming method has a significant impact on payback.

- **Minimal downtime**: OLP makes it possible to test and optimize programs on the PC while production continues. Virtual planning makes it possible to avoid collisions and reduce actual commissioning to just a few tests. Experience reports show that OLP requires up to 80% less programming time and increases robot utilization by up to 95%.
- **Flexibility and scalability**: No‑code systems and automatic programming allow adjustments within minutes. Employees without programming knowledge can create and adapt movement sequences. This enables a quick response to new products or seasonal fluctuations.
- **cost reduction**: Intuitive robot operation reduces training costs and dependency on specialized programmers. At the same time, process errors are minimized through simulation and automatic optimization, which reduces scrap costs.
- **Relief for staff**: AGVs and AMRs allow monotonous transport tasks to be automated. Thanks to intelligent navigation, AMRs can avoid obstacles and therefore work without interruption. Staff is freed up for higher-value tasks.
- **Security and compliance**: Compliance with EN ISO 3691‑4 and EN ISO 13849‑1 is mandatory. Redundant safety systems, defined warning and protection zones, and regular risk assessments ensure that people are protected. Emergency stop switches, protective fields, and lidar sensors prevent collisions.

‍

## Programming robots in practice

### Case study: Intralogistics at a medium-sized manufacturer

**challenge**: A medium-sized machine manufacturer in southern Germany had to move up to 1,500 material containers between storage, production and shipping every day. Manual driving with forklifts led to delays, safety risks and high personnel costs. At the same time, there was no know-how to program complex industrial robots yourself.

**solution**: EF Robotics first analyzed the material flow data and defined the requirements together with the customer. Then were **AGV robot programmed**, which cover fixed routes to pre-assembly sites. For the variable part of the warehouse, additional **AMR robot** used. Most of the robot programs were created offline; only fine adjustments were made in teach‑in mode. Thanks to digital twins, collision points were eliminated and the control systems were able to **PLC** be integrated. The programming took into account all relevant safety standards (warning and protection zones) and enabled the robots to easily interact with existing conveyor systems.

**upshot**: The system was ready for use within twelve weeks. Material transport times were significantly reduced and operators were able to concentrate on assembly tasks. The plant manager reported: _“Working with EF Robotics has shown us how important it is to have the right combination of offline programmed robotic logic and flexible AMR systems. After a short training session, our employees made adjustments themselves — this has significantly increased acceptance. ”_

### Customer voice

_“We respected robotics because programming overwhelmed us. EF Robotics has given us a_ **_No‑code approach_** _and let the robot be taught together with our team. After a few hours, we were able to adjust processes ourselves. Today, AGVs run around the clock and we can concentrate on our core competencies. ”_ — Production manager of a medium-sized logistics company

![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/67a0d1e7d87774056a9a2088/69b432d48ecad2a8046c3a9a_automatischer-transportwagen-mit-metallteilen-in-fabrik.webp)

## Expert tip from Ruwen Prochnow

**“This is a tip that we always give our customers: First define your process and the specific use case before you select the technology.** Without a proper analysis of material flows, security requirements and interfaces to your ERP or MES, you risk making expensive mistakes. We recommend using virtual simulations early on to see the impact of changes. Only when the process is stable do we select the appropriate robot hardware, control system and programming method. This keeps your solution cost-effective and scalable. ”

## Technical details & integration

### Programming languages and control systems

Industrial robots are programmed using proprietary languages such as URScript, KRL (KUKA) or RAPID (ABB). In practice, modern approaches combine various technologies:

- **Robot Operating System (ROS2)**: ROS2 provides a collection of libraries for navigation, perception, and AI. It is suitable for applications where the robot must interpret the environment. ROS2 uses DDS middleware, which enables real-time communication. Since ROS2 is not designed for tough real-time requirements, it is usually used as a “brain” during the **PLC** takes over deterministic control as a “muscle”. PLCs are robust industrial computers that process logic in milliseconds and thus ensure safety.
- **hybrid architectures**: Many modern systems combine PLC logic with ROS modules. Vision sensors, AI algorithms and digital twins run in ROS2, while safety functions and motion control are embedded in the PLC. Decision-makers should pay attention to open interfaces to connect both worlds.

### Security guidelines in programming

EN ISO 3691‑4 requires that AGVs must have at least two independent safety systems — a primary system for normal operation and a secondary system for emergencies. Safety zones must be defined: a warning zone reduces speed, a protection zone stops the vehicle immediately. EN ISO 13849‑1 lays down the **Performance level d** fixed; this means that the probability of a dangerous failure must be extremely low. Operators are required to carry out a risk assessment for every safety function and to arrange for regular tests. Germany has additional DGUV regulations; AGV systems must be inspected annually by specialists.

### Automatic programming and no‑code

The development of automatic program generators is revolutionizing robotics. OLP software can independently create movement paths from CAD data and defined quality criteria. Users define tolerances; the software performs path optimization and collision testing. Advantages: program-free creation, reduced errors and quick adjustments. Together with drag‑and‑drop systems, this allows operators with no code experience access to robotics.

### AGV and AMR integration

When integrating transport robots, decision makers must consider the differences between AGV and AMR. AGVs are suitable for consistent production routes. Their programming is based on fixed routes; obstacles are detected but the vehicles stop until the path is clear. AMRs are more flexible: They use SLAM algorithms and sensor fusion to navigate autonomously. In dynamic environments, they can avoid obstacles and choose alternative routes. For economic use, both systems should have a **Fleet Management** be controlled. This assigns transport orders by priority and allocates them efficiently.

### Control systems and IT integration

An efficient robot solution is only as good as its integration into existing IT and control systems. EF Robotics uses open interfaces **ERP**, **MES** and warehouse management systems. In this way, orders can be sent directly to robot fleets and status data can be reported back in real time. Data interfaces such as REST APIs, OPC UA or Modbus TCP connect PLC controllers with higher-level systems. The following applies to safety functions: Emergency off signals and light barriers are connected to the PLC on the hardware side, while ROS2 modules for navigation and AI are protected by redundant safety channels.

## Implementation with EF Robotics

[Roboter Highlights: EF Robotics auf der GastRo Messe - Tag 2](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ALgMfbMTPr8) [EF Robotics](https://www.youtube.com/channel/UC_04uCeFssL12MJWAcO7GZA)

EF Robotics265 subscribers

### Analysis & advice

[EF Robotics](https://www.ef-robotics.de/uber-ef-robotics) Every project starts with a **procedural analysis**. Material flows, cycle times, safety requirements and layouts are recorded. The vendor-independent advice helps to objectively consider whether a **Programming industrial robots**, an AGV or an AMR is the best solution. In addition to **Robotics programming** alternatives such as process optimization and organizational measures have also been examined.

### Selection of suitable robotics

Based on the analysis, EF Robotics selects from a broad portfolio of robots, controllers, and software. For **Programming industrial robots** Established brands (KUKA, FANUC, ABB) are just as suitable as new providers of collaborative robotics. In the case of transport solutions, AGVs or AMRs from different manufacturers are assessed. It is important that control systems meet the requirements and can be integrated into existing IT. Die **Security guidelines programming** are taken into account from the outset.

### Integration into existing processes

Integration is iterative: First, the robot program is developed offline and tested with the customer in simulations. This is followed by _Teachen_ the detailed steps on site. EF Robotics connects robot programs with PLC controls and ERP systems. **Robotics control systems** are configured so that emergency stop signals, sensors and fleet management work smoothly. The customer receives documentation that covers normative standards and risk assessments.

### Start-up, training & service

After implementation, commissioning is carried out with safety approval. EF Robotics trains operators and technical personnel to use teach‑in devices, OLP software, and the fleet manager. Training courses are aimed at different target groups — from line workers to IT administrators. In the long term, EF Robotics supports its customers with maintenance, software updates and extensions. In this way, the solution remains scalable and sustainable.

![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/67a0d1e7d87774056a9a2088/69b4340dbe9d44ec4c067212_roboter-mit-bildschirm-im-lager.webp)

Die **Robot programming** It's much more than writing code today. It includes the structured analysis of processes, the selection of suitable technologies and consistent compliance with safety standards. Modern methods such as offline programming, automatic program creation, and **No‑code approaches** shorten project runtimes and make robotics accessible even without a large IT team. AGVs and AMRs perform transport tasks, while industrial robots automate precise manufacturing steps. Decision-makers benefit from shorter downtime, lower costs and a high level of flexibility. EF Robotics stands as a vendor-independent consultant, integrator and **Long-term partners** ready to plan, program and securely operate your automation processes. Arrange a non-binding [Consultation](https://www.ef-robotics.de/kontakt) to take the first step towards a more efficient future.

## **We would be happy to advise you.**

Our team has experience using robots in many areas. Make an appointment and we will be happy to advise you.
